papers.title: Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, Rock (2023) — GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models (arXiv 2303.10130)
papers.verified: Fetched https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.10130 (abstract, verbatim) and https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2303.10130 (body text). All quotes below returned verbatim from those fetches.

key_numbers.quantity: Share of US workforce with >=10% of work tasks exposed to LLMs
key_numbers.quote: around 80% of the U.S. workforce could have at least 10% of their work tasks affected by the introduction of LLMs

key_numbers.quantity: Share of workers with >=50% of tasks exposed
key_numbers.quote: approximately 19% of workers may see at least 50% of their tasks impacted

key_numbers.quantity: Share of ALL US worker tasks exposed to LLM alone (alpha = E1: time to complete cut >=50% at equal quality)
key_numbers.quote: about 15% of all worker tasks in the US could be completed significantly faster at the same level of quality

key_numbers.quantity: Share of all tasks exposed once LLM-powered software is built (zeta = E1+E2)
key_numbers.quote: When incorporating software and tooling built on top of LLMs, this share increases to between 47 and 56% of all tasks.

key_numbers.quantity: Mean occupation-level direct exposure (alpha), human and GPT-4 raters
key_numbers.quote: Both human and GPT-4 annotations indicate that average occupation-level α values fall between 0.14 and 0.15

key_numbers.quantity: Wage gradient of exposure
key_numbers.value: positive (higher-income jobs more exposed)
key_numbers.quote: The projected effects span all wage levels, with higher-income jobs potentially facing greater exposure to LLM capabilities and LLM-powered software.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.30 central; 0.15 (conservative) to 0.50-0.55 (aggressive ceiling)
lever_mappings.rationale: alpha (bare-LLM, time-halved) = 15% of all tasks and mean occupation alpha 0.14-0.15 -> lower bound 0.15. zeta (with LLM-built software) = 47-56% of all tasks -> ceiling ~0.50-0.55. beta = E1 + 0.5*E2 sits near 0.30 and is the natural central anchor. Note: exposure means >=50% time reduction, not full task elimination; if the model interprets cognitive_feasibility as fully-automatable share, apply a further discount (e.g. x0.5-0.7) to these figures.

lever_mappings.suggested: no anchor from this paper; LLM channel contributes ~0
lever_mappings.rationale: The rubric scores only whether an LLM or LLM-powered software cuts task time; occupations with zero exposed tasks are overwhelmingly manual (agricultural equipment operators, dishwashers, athletes per Appendix D). Anchor physical_feasibility from robotics sources instead, not from LLM exposure.

lever_mappings.suggested: start ~0.27-0.32 of the feasibility ceiling; end -> 1.0; timeline exogenous
lever_mappings.rationale: alpha/zeta = 15% / (47-56%) = 0.27-0.32: only ~30% of ultimately-exposed task value is deliverable through the bare LLM interface; the rest requires complementary software investment, which is exactly what a rising cumulative adoption ceiling represents. The paper explicitly refuses a timeline ("We do not make predictions about the development or adoption timeline of such LLMs"), so n_periods must come from diffusion literature, not here.

lever_mappings.suggested: >=0.3 for cognitively displaced (direction only)
lever_mappings.rationale: Exposure rises with income, so displaced cognitive workers come disproportionately from above-median wages. If re-employment is at a service-floor wage around a state p30, haircut ~ 1 - w_p30/w_origin >= 0.3-0.45. This is a distributional implication of the exposure gradient, not a number the paper estimates.

papers.caveats: Exposure is a TIME-SAVINGS potential (>=50% faster at equal quality), agnostic between substitution and augmentation — it is not a displacement share and not adoption; mapping zeta directly to cognitive_feasibility is an upper bound. No timeline is offered, so it says nothing about adoption_path speed, reabsorption_rate, or any fiscal lever. GPT-4 partially labels its own exposure (circularity); human-vs-model agreement is only moderate at the beta level. Physical work is excluded by construction. Disagreement note: Eloundou exposure rises with wages but flattens/dips at the very top (Job Zone 4 beta ~34.5% > Job Zone 5 ~26.45% per fetched Table 6 values), unlike Webb whose AI exposure peaks sharply at ~p90; unlike Felten, it yields absolute task-share units usable as levels.

papers.title: Felten, Raj, Seamans (2023) — How will Language Modelers like ChatGPT Affect Occupations and Industries? (arXiv 2303.01157 / SSRN 4375268; LM-weighted AIOE)
papers.verified: Fetched https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.01157 (abstract) and full PDF https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.01157 (downloaded and text-extracted locally, including the 774-occupation appendix). All quotes verbatim from extracted text (ligatures/spacing normalized).

key_numbers.quantity: Correlation of language-modeling AIOE with original all-application AIOE
key_numbers.quote: The resulting scores are highly correlated with the original AIOE scores (correlation coefficient: 0.979).

key_numbers.quantity: Wage-exposure relationship (708 occupations matched to BLS 2021 wages)
key_numbers.value: positive, statistically significant (no elasticity coefficient reported)
key_numbers.quote: We also find a positive and statistically significant correlation between an occupation's mean or median wage and our measure of exposure to AI language modeling.

key_numbers.quantity: Top-ranked occupation, LM-AIOE (standardized index)
key_numbers.value: Telemarketers, 1.926
key_numbers.quote: 1 41-9041 Telemarketers 1.926

key_numbers.quantity: Bottom-ranked occupation, LM-AIOE (rank 774 of 774)
key_numbers.value: Pressers, Textile, Garment: -1.854
key_numbers.quote: 774 51-6021 Pressers, Textile, Garment, and Related Materials -1.854

key_numbers.quantity: Measure construction scale
key_numbers.value: 10 AI applications x 52 O*NET abilities over 800+ occupations
key_numbers.quote: O*NET uses these 52 human abilities to describe the occupational makeup of each of 800+ occupations that it tracks.

key_numbers.quantity: Sample matched to wages
key_numbers.value: 708 occupations
key_numbers.quote: We have 708 occupations present in both datasets.

lever_mappings.suggested: no LEVEL anchor — use LM-AIOE only as cross-occupation weights in the exposure mapping
lever_mappings.rationale: LM-AIOE is a standardized relative index (range roughly -1.85 to +1.93 across 774 occupations) with no 0-1 'share of tasks automatable' units. Correct use: fix the aggregate cognitive_feasibility level from Eloundou-type task shares, then distribute it across occupations/wage bins proportional to LM-AIOE rank so the displaced wage bill has the right composition.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.3-0.5 for LM-exposed displaced workers
lever_mappings.rationale: Positive significant wage-exposure correlation over 708 occupations means the displaced pool skews white-collar and above-median wage (top-20 dominated by post-secondary teachers, legal, finance). Falling to a service-floor wage around a state p25-p35 from origin wages around p60-p80 implies proportional cuts of roughly 1 - w_p30/w_p70 ~ 0.3-0.5.

lever_mappings.suggested: allow both signs in Felten-anchored presets, e.g. -0.1 to +0.1
lever_mappings.rationale: The AIOE is explicitly agnostic: exposure 'could involve substitution or augmentation depending on various factors associated with the occupation itself.' A preset anchored on this measure should not force a negative (substitution-only) survivor wage response; the augmentation branch corresponds to positive survivor_elasticity.

papers.caveats: The paper provides NO displacement magnitudes, no task shares, no adoption rates, and no fiscal quantities — every 0-1 level lever is out of reach; it only orders occupations/industries/geographies by relative exposure. The wage relationship is shown in binned scatter form; no regression coefficient is reported in the text, so it anchors direction, not magnitude. 'Exposure' deliberately conflates substitution with augmentation, so mapping it to displacement (cognitive_feasibility x adoption) overstates job loss under the augmentation branch. Disagreement note: its LM-specific top list (telemarketers, English/foreign-language/history teachers; legal services, securities) differs from Webb's pre-LLM AI peak (lab technicians, chemical engineers, optometrists at ~p90) and its 0.979 correlation with the all-application AIOE means the LM re-weighting barely changes the ranking — so its 'ChatGPT-specific' claim rests mostly on the original 2021 crowd-sourced matrix.

papers.title: Webb (2020) — The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Labor Market (SSRN 3482150; patent-task text overlap measure)
papers.verified: Full paper PDF fetched from https://www.michaelwebb.co/webb_ai.pdf (61 pages, downloaded and text-extracted locally). All quotes verbatim from extracted text (ligatures normalized).

key_numbers.quantity: Robot exposure, 25th->75th percentile: within-industry employment and wage effects, 1980-2010
key_numbers.value: employment share -9 to -18%; wages -8 to -14%
key_numbers.quote: moving from the 25th to the 75th percentile of exposure to robots is associated with a decline in within-industry employment shares of between 9 and 18%, and a decline in wages of between 8 and 14%, depending on the specification

key_numbers.quantity: Software exposure, 25th->75th percentile: employment and wage effects, 1980-2010
key_numbers.value: employment -7 to -11%; wages -2 to -6%
key_numbers.quote: For software, the magnitudes are smaller, with declines of 7-11% and 2-6%, respectively.

key_numbers.quantity: Location of peak AI exposure in the wage distribution
key_numbers.value: ~90th percentile
key_numbers.quote: I find that high-skill occupations are most exposed to AI, with exposure peaking at about the ninetieth percentile.

key_numbers.quantity: Projected AI effect on 90:10 wage inequality
key_numbers.value: -4% (software coefficient) to -9% (robot coefficient)
key_numbers.quote: Applying the estimated software coefficient to AI, I project a 4% decrease in 90:10 inequality; using the robot coefficient, the decrease is 9%.

key_numbers.quantity: Skill direction of AI exposure
key_numbers.value: high-skilled tasks; top 1% unaffected
key_numbers.quote: I find that, in contrast to software and robots, AI is directed at high-skilled tasks. Under the assumption that historical patterns of long-run substitution will continue, I estimate that AI will reduce 90:10 wage inequality, but will not affect the top 1%.

key_numbers.quantity: Education gradient of AI exposure
key_numbers.value: college and Master's degree holders most exposed
key_numbers.quote: While individuals with low levels of education are somewhat exposed to AI, it is those with college degrees, including Master's degrees, who are most exposed.

lever_mappings.suggested: negative, modest: annualized wage drag ~0.1-0.5%/yr per interquartile exposure gap
lever_mappings.rationale: Occupation-level wage declines for exposed-but-surviving occupations: robots -8 to -14% and software -2 to -6% over ~30 years (1980-2010) -> 0.07-0.5%/yr. Supports a negative (substitution) sign and small magnitude for survivors' market wage response in exposed occupations under a 'historical-pace' preset.

lever_mappings.suggested: 10-30 years (central ~15-20)
lever_mappings.rationale: The robot case study's full employment/wage effect accrues over a 30-year window (1980-2010); the physical channel's ramp-in is measured in decades. A linear ramp reaching full effect in ~15-20 years is a reasonable compression of that history for a modern preset; 10 years is aggressive relative to it.

lever_mappings.suggested: historical-pace validation target: cumulative displacement in top-quartile-exposed occupations ~9-18% over 30 years (~0.3-0.6%/yr)
lever_mappings.rationale: The robot IQR employment decline of 9-18% over 30y is a realized joint outcome of feasibility x adoption; a Webb-anchored 'history repeats' preset should have adoption_path x feasibility deliver cumulative displacement of that order for the most exposed slice, far below the Eloundou feasibility ceiling.

lever_mappings.suggested: elevated for AI-displaced (origin wages near p90): ~0.4-0.6 if re-employment is at a service floor
lever_mappings.rationale: AI exposure peaks at ~p90 of the wage distribution and among college/Master's holders, so Webb-anchored displaced workers have the highest origin wages of the three papers; 1 - w_p30/w_p90 lands around 0.5+. Direction/composition only — Webb estimates equilibrium occupation wages, not individual displacement losses.

lever_mappings.suggested: no 0-1 level; distribution weight: up-weight p70-p95 wage occupations in the exposure mapping
lever_mappings.rationale: Webb's measure is a percentile-ranked patent-task overlap, unitless like AIOE; it cannot set the automatable share. Its contribution is the shape: exposure peaking at ~p90 implies the displaced cognitive wage bill per worker (and hence forgone income/payroll tax per displaced worker) is well above the economy average under a Webb-weighted mapping.

papers.caveats: Concept gaps: (1) exposure scores are employment-weighted PERCENTILES (relative, unitless) — no lever level can be read off; (2) the 9-18%/8-14% effects are within-industry occupation equilibrium changes over 30 years, not individual displacement events, so they map poorly to reemployment_haircut and reabsorption_rate (which are person-level) and cannot anchor demand_multiplier (they are relative declines, not net national job losses); (3) explicitly non-causal ('Although I cannot attribute causality to the exposure scores'); (4) AI is measured from pre-2020 patents (prediction/detection/optimization) and predates LLMs, so it misses generative-AI exposure of writing-heavy occupations. Disagreement note: Webb peaks at ~p90 (lab technicians, chemical engineers, optometrists, power plant operators) and finds older, college/Master's-educated workers most exposed; Eloundou finds broad exposure rising with wage but flattening at the very top job zone; Felten's LM index tops out with telemarketers and post-secondary teachers. Presets should treat the exposure MAPPING (which wage bins get hit) as the main axis of disagreement across the three papers, holding the aggregate feasibility level (from Eloundou) fixed.

papers.title: Acemoglu (2024), "The Simple Macroeconomics of AI" (NBER WP 32487 / Economic Policy)
papers.verified: Fetched and verified: https://www.nber.org/papers/w32487 (abstract, NBER version) and https://economics.mit.edu/sites/default/files/2024-04/The%20Simple%20Macroeconomics%20of%20AI.pdf (full text, April 2024 MIT draft; text extracted from downloaded PDF). Body quotes come from the MIT draft, whose headline bounds (0.71%/0.55%) differ slightly from the NBER abstract (0.66%/0.53%).

key_numbers.quantity: 10-year aggregate TFP gain from AI, upper bound (baseline and hard-task-adjusted)
key_numbers.value: <=0.66% baseline, <0.53% adjusted (NBER version); 0.71% / 0.55% in April 2024 MIT draft
key_numbers.quote: these macroeconomic effects appear nontrivial but modest—no more than a 0.66% increase in total factor productivity (TFP) over 10 years. [...] Consequently, predicted TFP gains over the next 10 years are even more modest and are predicted to be less than 0.53%.

key_numbers.quantity: Share of US labor tasks exposed to AI (wage-bill weighted, from Eloundou et al.)
key_numbers.value: 19.9% of the US wage bill / GDP share of tasks
key_numbers.quote: This procedure yields a wage bill-weighted share of exposed occupations equal to 19.9%. I interpret this number to be the same as the GDP share of tasks exposed to AI.

key_numbers.quantity: Fraction of exposed tasks profitably automatable within 10 years (Svanberg et al. extrapolation) and implied impacted GDP share
key_numbers.value: 23% of exposed tasks; 0.23 x 0.199 = 4.6% of all tasks impacted within 10 years (sensitivity: 23% -> ~30%)
key_numbers.quote: Svanberg et al.'s base estimates imply that among computer vision-exposed tasks, 23% can be feasibly (and profitably) automated within 10 years. Applying this number to Eloundou et al.'s estimates, I arrive at the GDP share of tasks impacted by AI as 0.23 x 0.199 = 4.6% of all tasks (or occupations) will be impacted by AI and computer vision technologies within the next 10 years.

key_numbers.quantity: Task-level cost savings: labor cost savings and total cost savings after labor-share conversion
key_numbers.value: 27% labor cost savings; x 0.57 exposure-adjusted labor share = 15.4% total cost savings (20.5% if Peng et al. included)
key_numbers.quote: Under these assumptions, the average labor cost savings are 27% (= 0.27). [...] This gives an average (AI exposure-adjusted) labor share of 0.57, and thus the average (overall) cost savings from AI are about 0.27 x 0.57 = 0.154.

key_numbers.quantity: 10-year GDP gain including capital deepening (baseline, upper bound, and hard-task-adjusted)
key_numbers.value: ~1.1% (capital proportional to TFP); 1.6-1.8% upper bound (Acemoglu-Restrepo 2022 investment); 0.90-0.92% with hard-task adjustment
key_numbers.quote: With these investment effects incorporated, GDP is also estimated to grow by around 1.1% over the next 10 years. [...] the upper bound on GDP effects rises to 1.6-1.8%. Nevertheless, my framework also clarifies that what is relevant for consumer welfare is TFP, rather than GDP, since the additional investment comes out of consumption.

key_numbers.quantity: Distributional result: capital vs labor split of the gains; wage effects
key_numbers.value: capital share of national income +0.38 pp; no sizable wage rises; real-wage declines only for low-education (esp. white native-born) women
key_numbers.quote: GDP therefore increases substantially more than average wages, and as a result, the capital share of national income increases by about 0.38 percentage points. This confirms that inequality between capital and labor is likely to rise as a result of the rollout of AI.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.20 (upper bound; conservative preset 0.15-0.20)
lever_mappings.rationale: Eloundou et al. exposure aggregated by Acemoglu to a 19.9% wage-bill-weighted share of US tasks exposed to AI. Direct read-across: the exposed share of cognitive task content = 0.199 ~ 0.20. Treat as a CEILING: Acemoglu stresses the exposure measure 'contains both automation and task complementarity elements', so pure automatable content is strictly below 0.20.

lever_mappings.suggested: start ~0.02 -> end 0.23 with n_periods = 10 (sensitivity: end 0.30)
lever_mappings.rationale: Svanberg et al. (extrapolated by Acemoglu from computer vision to all exposed tasks): only 23% of exposed tasks are profitably automated within 10 years. Since the model's adoption_path is the cumulative share of FEASIBLE work actually automated by year t, set the 10-year endpoint to 0.23 with cognitive_feasibility = 0.20, reproducing his impacted share 0.23 x 0.199 = 4.6%. His sensitivity (faster cost declines raise the feasible fraction 'from 23% to approximately 30%') gives end = 0.30.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.12-0.155 (0.155 baseline; 0.12 hard-task-adjusted)
lever_mappings.rationale: Hulten arithmetic: TFP gain = impacted GDP share x total cost savings = 0.046 x 0.154 = 0.71%. The automated wage bill in the model at year 10 is cognitive_feasibility x adoption_end = 0.199 x 0.23 = 4.6% of the labor bill, so the GDP dividend per unit of automated labor bill is 0.0071/0.046 = 0.154 -> productivity_passthrough ~ 0.155 (full automation of the labor bill -> +15.5% of GDP). The hard-task discount scales the average cost savings by 0.55/0.71 = 0.775 -> ~0.12. With these settings the model's 10-year GDP dividend reproduces Acemoglu's 0.53-0.71% TFP validation target.

lever_mappings.suggested: survivor_gains_share ~ 0.00-0.05; profit-weighted remainder, e.g. 0.60/0.35/0.05 (and survivor_elasticity ~ 0)
lever_mappings.rationale: Acemoglu: 'productivity gains from AI are unlikely to lead to sizable wage rises' and the capital share of national income rises ~0.38 pp because 'GDP therefore increases substantially more than average wages' — i.e., the saved bill accrues to capital, not to survivor raises, making the survivor channel second-order in the conservative preset. Some price_reduction_share (~0.3-0.4) is consistent with his mechanism operating through task-level cost/price declines under competition, but the distributional result caps the wage share of the disposition near zero.

lever_mappings.suggested: ~0 (0-0.3) in the conservative preset
lever_mappings.rationale: The entire quantitative apparatus is first-order Hulten: reallocation/demand-composition terms 'will be second-order and can be ignored in computing GDP changes' at his shock size (4.6% of tasks over 10 years), and his GE exercise finds AI 'is unlikely to lead to substantial wage declines for affected groups'. A conservative Acemoglu preset therefore turns off Okun-style induced job-loss amplification (demand_multiplier ~ 0).

papers.caveats: (1) No displacement dynamics: the framework holds employment fixed (the underlying experiments 'do not see the displacement effects'), so the paper offers NO anchors for reabsorption_rate, reemployment_haircut, ui_weeks, lfp_exit_rate, attrition_rate, or ssdi_annual — the conservative preset must take those from displacement literature (e.g., Davis-von Wachter). (2) Exposure is not automation: the 19.9% bundles automation with task complementarities, so mapping it to cognitive_feasibility overstates displacement even while bounding GDP gains; a preset that uses 0.20 feasibility x 0.23 adoption for DISPLACED headcount is more aggressive on job loss than Acemoglu's own reading. (3) Cost-savings concept mismatch: his 27% is a partial labor-cost reduction on tasks where humans remain (speed gains), not a saved full wage bill; the model saves ~100% of automated compensation, so calibrate through productivity_passthrough only and do not also tune auto_cost to hit 27% — that would double-count. (4) The 23%-profitable figure is a computer-vision estimate extrapolated to all exposed tasks; it is a weak anchor for physical_feasibility/robotics_lag (his numbers are essentially a cognitive+vision pool; a conservative preset can set physical_feasibility low with robotics_lag >= 10y, but that is interpretation, not his estimate). (5) Version drift: NBER/Economic Policy abstract says 0.66%/0.53% TFP; the fetched April 2024 MIT draft says 0.71%/0.55% (GDP 1.1%, hard-task 0.90-0.92%) — use the ranges, not point values. (6) He argues welfare-relevant gains are TFP, not GDP: the 1.6-1.8% GDP upper bound is investment-driven and 'comes out of consumption', so it should not be fed into the model as a demand boost; likewise his 'bad new tasks' example (GDP +2%, welfare -0.72%) has no lever counterpart. (7) No compute-tax, offshore, UBI, or state-budget content: compute_effective_rate, offshore_share, automation_tax_rate, ubi_*, state_* levers get nothing from this paper.

papers.title: Brynjolfsson, Li, Raymond (2025), "Generative AI at Work", Quarterly Journal of Economics 140(2): 889-942 (NBER WP 31161, 2023)
papers.verified: Fetched: https://www.nber.org/papers/w31161 (working-paper abstract); https://ideas.repec.org/a/oup/qjecon/v140y2025i2p889-942..html (published QJE abstract); http://danielle.li/assets/docs/GenerativeAIatWork.pdf (full published text, downloaded and text-extracted). All quotes below verified against fetched text.

key_numbers.quantity: Average productivity gain from AI assistance (published QJE version)
key_numbers.value: +15% issues resolved per hour
key_numbers.quote: AI assistance increases worker productivity, resulting in a 15% increase in the number of chats that an agent successfully resolves per hour.

key_numbers.quantity: Productivity gain for least-skilled/least-experienced workers
key_numbers.value: +30% issues resolved per hour
key_numbers.quote: Less skilled and less experienced workers improve significantly across all productivity measures, including a 30% increase in the number of issues resolved per hour.

key_numbers.quantity: Experience-curve compression (tenure equivalence)
key_numbers.value: 2 months with AI = 6+ months without
key_numbers.quote: The AI tool also helps newer agents to move more quickly down the experience curve: treated agents with two months of tenure perform just as well as untreated agents with more than six months of tenure.

key_numbers.quantity: Effect on highest-skilled workers
key_numbers.value: ~0 productivity gain; small quality decline
key_numbers.quote: In contrast, AI has little effect on the productivity of higher-skilled or more experienced workers. Indeed, we find evidence that AI assistance leads to a small decrease in the quality of conversations conducted by the most skilled agents.

key_numbers.quantity: Attrition reduction for newer agents with AI access
key_numbers.value: -10pp, i.e. -40% vs 25% baseline attrition
key_numbers.quote: The magnitude of this coefficient, around 10 percentage points, translates into a 40% decrease relative to a baseline attrition rate in this group of 25%.

key_numbers.quantity: Working-paper (NBER 2023) version of the headline numbers
key_numbers.value: +14% average, +34% novice/low-skilled
key_numbers.quote: Access to the tool increases productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 14% on average ... including a 34% improvement for novice and low-skilled workers but with minimal impact on experienced and highly skilled workers

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.5-0.9 (central ~0.7) for the cognitive channel
lever_mappings.rationale: A 15% rise in resolutions/hour with unchanged labor input means unit labor requirements fall by 1 - 1/1.15 = 13%, and that saving shows up as real, measured output at the task level (micro passthrough ~1.0). Discount to 0.5-0.9 at GDP level for non-scalable tasks, demand constraints, and the J-curve measurement lag; use the 15% (avg) to 30% (novice) range as the bound on what current-generation genAI delivers per exposed job.

The productivity surplus is created disproportionately by novice survivors (+30%) but the study shows no contemporaneous pay increase for agents — the firm captures the surplus in the deployment window. So of the saved wage bill, only a small slice flows to survivor raises; the residual belongs in retained_profit_share / price_reduction_share.

lever_mappings.suggested: +0.05 to +0.2 (complementarity sign) at low automation depth
lever_mappings.rationale: AI raised low-skill survivor productivity 30% and cut their attrition ~40%, i.e., current genAI complements remaining cognitive-service workers rather than substituting for them; the elasticity sign should start positive (or ~0) in early-adoption presets, flipping negative only as depth increases.

lever_mappings.suggested: low end, ~0.10-0.20, for re-employment into AI-assisted service work
lever_mappings.rationale: Tenure premium compression: 2 months with AI = 6+ months without means the seniority/experience component of wages is compressed ~3x in AI-assisted jobs, so displaced workers re-entering service-floor jobs lose less occupation-specific human capital than historical displacement studies (which imply 0.15-0.30) would suggest.

lever_mappings.suggested: floor of ~0.15-0.30 of task content in fully cognitive occupations
lever_mappings.rationale: The assistant already substitutes for 13-23% of unit labor requirements (1-1/1.15 to 1-1/1.30) in customer support, a bellwether cognitive occupation, using 2020-2021-era models; treat this as a lower bound on currently automatable cognitive task share, not a point estimate.

papers.caveats: This is an AUGMENTATION study: no worker was displaced, so it does not directly identify displacement levers (adoption_path, reabsorption_rate, lfp_exit_rate). The 15%/30% gains are within one firm, one occupation (customer support), one tool — external validity to the whole cognitive wage bill is an assumption. The attrition result (workers staying longer) is the opposite concept from the model's attrition_rate (exit of long-term unemployed) — do not map it there. The reemployment_haircut mapping is an inference from experience-curve compression, not a measured wage change. Published QJE version says 15%/30%; the 2023 NBER working paper said 14%/34% — presets should cite the published numbers.

papers.title: Brynjolfsson, Chandar, Chen (2025), "Canaries in the Coal Mine? Six Facts about the Recent Employment Effects of Artificial Intelligence", Stanford Digital Economy Lab / SIEPR working paper (Nov 2025 revision)
papers.verified: Fetched: https://digitaleconomy.stanford.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/Canaries_BrynjolfssonChandarChen.pdf (full PDF downloaded and text-extracted; the file at this URL is the November 2025 revision, which updated the headline from 13% to 16%). All quotes verified against extracted text.

key_numbers.quantity: Headline relative employment decline, ages 22-25 in AI-exposed occupations (controlling for firm shocks)
key_numbers.value: -16% relative
key_numbers.quote: Early-career workers (ages 22-25) in AI-exposed occupations experienced 16% relative employment declines, controlling for firm-level shocks, while employment for experienced workers remained stable.

key_numbers.quantity: Absolute employment change, most-exposed occupations, late 2022 to Sept 2025
key_numbers.value: -6% (ages 22-25) vs +6-9% (older workers)
key_numbers.quote: workers aged 22 to 25 have experienced a 6% decline in employment from late 2022 to September 2025 in the most AI-exposed occupations, compared to a 6-9% increase for older workers

key_numbers.quantity: Young software developers, peak-to-Sept-2025
key_numbers.quote: By September 2025, employment for software developers aged 22-25 declined nearly 20% compared to its peak in late 2022.

key_numbers.quantity: Event-study estimate, ages 22-25, top vs bottom exposure quintile
key_numbers.value: -15 log points
key_numbers.quote: For workers aged 22-25, we find a 15 log-point decline in relative employment for the most AI-exposed quintiles compared to the least exposed quintile, a large and statistically significant effect.

key_numbers.quantity: Adjustment margin: employment vs wages
key_numbers.value: ~0 differential salary trend by exposure
key_numbers.quote: Fifth, the labor market adjustments are visible in employment more than in compensation. In contrast to our findings for employment, we find little difference in annual salary trends by age or exposure quintile, suggesting possible wage stickiness.

key_numbers.quantity: Automation vs augmentation split
key_numbers.value: declines only where AI automates; growth where it augments
key_numbers.quote: While we find employment declines for young workers in occupations where AI primarily automates work, we find employment growth in occupations in which AI use is most augmentative.

lever_mappings.suggested: cumulative value ~0.01-0.03 at year 3 of the cognitive channel (i.e., start of the path near 0.01, not 0.05+)
lever_mappings.rationale: Late 2022 to Sept 2025 is ~3 years. Ages 22-25 are roughly 8-10% of headcount in exposed occupations; they fell ~6% absolute while older workers grew 6-9%, so NET occupation-level displacement so far is ~0-1% of exposed-occupation employment. With cognitive_feasibility ~0.3-0.5 for those occupations, that is ~1-3% of the feasible pool actually automated after 3 years — a slow-start S-curve, operating via hiring freezes at the entry margin.

lever_mappings.suggested: ~0 for the first 3-5 model years
lever_mappings.rationale: Fact 5: adjustment is on the employment margin, with 'little difference in annual salary trends by age or exposure quintile' — wage stickiness means incumbent survivor wages should not respond to slack in early years; phase in any negative substitution elasticity only after the sticky-wage window.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.25-0.45, concentrated in the top two exposure quintiles
lever_mappings.rationale: Effects appear only in the highest two of five Eloundou-exposure quintiles (~40% of occupations) and, within those, only where Anthropic-usage data classifies AI use as automative rather than augmentative — so the technically-automatable-now share of cognitive task content is well below 1 and the augmentative remainder should not be counted as feasible displacement.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.2-0.4/yr in early-period presets
lever_mappings.rationale: Aggregate employment 'continues to grow robustly' and less-exposed occupations grew 5-13% over the same 3 years, implying displaced/never-hired young workers are largely absorbed elsewhere while displacement volume is still small; supports a relatively high early reabsorption_rate that presets can degrade as displacement scales.

lever_mappings.suggested: do not anchor from this paper; keep whatever value other displacement literature gives
lever_mappings.rationale: The paper finds essentially no compensation response at all ('little difference in compensation trends by age or exposure quintile'), which is evidence of short-run wage stickiness, not of a zero haircut for displaced workers who actually churn to new jobs — ADP salary data covers the employed, not the re-employed after displacement.

papers.caveats: The 16% (and 15 log-point) figure is a RELATIVE decline (vs least-exposed quintile / older workers) for one age band (22-25), not an aggregate displacement rate — mapping it directly onto adoption_path would overstate displacement by roughly an order of magnitude; use the net headcount arithmetic instead and treat the 16% as a validation target for the model's exposed-young-worker cohort, if the model tracked cohorts (it does not — it is age-aggregated). Displacement operates through reduced hiring, not layoffs, so it does not generate UI claimants the way the model's displacement flow does (ui_weeks, lfp_exit_rate get no anchor here). Data is ADP client firms (may not be nationally representative). The August 2025 draft's headline was 13%; the November 2025 revision at the same URL says 16% — cite the version explicitly in presets.

papers.title: Brynjolfsson, Rock, Syverson (2021), "The Productivity J-Curve: How Intangibles Complement General Purpose Technologies", AEJ: Macroeconomics 13(1): 333-372 (NBER WP 25148)
papers.verified: Fetched: https://www.nber.org/papers/w25148 (abstract); https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/mac.20180386 (journal abstract); https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w25148/w25148.pdf (full working-paper text downloaded and text-extracted). All quotes verified against fetched text.

key_numbers.quantity: TFP understatement from omitted intangibles
key_numbers.quote: Our adjusted measure TFP is 11.3% higher than official measures at the end of 2004, and 15.9% higher than official measures at the end of 2017.

key_numbers.quantity: Market value per $1 of observable IT investment (intangible multiplier basis)
key_numbers.value: ~$12 (s.e. $4); paper uses multiplier of 10
key_numbers.quote: we are guided by the Brynjolfsson, Hitt, and Yang (2002), who estimate that each unit of observable software and computer hardware is associated with roughly $12 (standard error $4) of firm market value

key_numbers.quantity: Correlated intangible co-investment per unit of tangible AI investment (bound to explain GDP slowdown)
key_numbers.value: ~2.7-4.1x observable investment
key_numbers.quote: correlated intangible investments per unit of tangible investment must be between roughly 2.7 and 4.1 times the observable investment values

key_numbers.quantity: Unmeasured intangible investment needed to explain the entire 2010-2017 GDP growth slowdown
key_numbers.quote: unmeasured intangible capital investment over 2010 to 2017 would need to average $107 billion per year (= 19.5 trillion * [2.72% - 2.17%]) in 2017 dollars to explain the entire slowdown

key_numbers.quantity: Baseline real GDP growth anchors
key_numbers.quote: Gross Domestic Product in the U.S. in 2017 was $19.5 trillion and in real terms grew at an average annual rate of 2.17% over 2010 to 2017, down from 2.72% per year from 2000 to 2007

key_numbers.quantity: AI-specific J-curve position (as of writing)
key_numbers.value: small but growing
key_numbers.quote: We then assess how AI-related intangible capital may be currently affecting measured productivity and find the effects are small but growing.

lever_mappings.suggested: 8-12 years (range 5-15) as the adoption-to-realized-output analog
lever_mappings.rationale: The software/hardware J-curve built from the 1990s and had 'not waned' by 2017, with adjusted-vs-official TFP gaps still widening 13-25 years in (11.3% in 2004 -> 15.9% in 2017); the complementary-capacity buildout that robotics_lag proxies (robot fleets + process redesign) is exactly the intangible co-investment phase, so a ramp of about a decade is the literature-anchored default rather than the 2-4 years optimists assume.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.3-0.6 of saved compensation in early/buildout years, declining thereafter
lever_mappings.rationale: Intangible co-investment runs ~2.7-4.1x observable tangible AI investment (and up to ~$10-12 of total value per $1 of measured IT spend), so the true all-in automation cost is a multiple of the visible compute bill; in the model's terms a large fraction of the saved wage bill is consumed by automation inputs during the ramp, shrinking the disposable surplus available for profit/prices/raises early on.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.4-0.7 measured in the first ~decade, ramping toward 0.9-1.0 later
lever_mappings.rationale: The J-curve says MEASURED GDP understates true output during the intangible buildout (official TFP understated by 15.9% by 2017) and overstates it during harvest; since the model's fiscal denominators use measured GDP, presets should phase passthrough in over ~10 years rather than apply a flat value — pair a low early value with a high late value instead of a single mid number.

lever_mappings.suggested: ~4.2-4.7% nominal (2.2-2.7% real + ~2% inflation)
lever_mappings.rationale: Direct anchor: real GDP growth averaged 2.17%/yr (2010-2017) and 2.72%/yr (2000-2007); take the pre/post-slowdown band as the real trend and add the Fed's 2% target for the nominal rate used in %-GDP denominators.

lever_mappings.suggested: low end, ~0.05-0.15 effective, during the buildout phase
lever_mappings.rationale: The complementary investments are 'often intangible and poorly measured in the national accounts' and are largely expensed rather than capitalized, so automation spending immediately shields corporate profit from tax; the effective rate collected on the compute-capital pool during the ramp sits well below the statutory rate that would apply to an equivalent tangible capital stock.

papers.caveats: The J-curve is a MEASUREMENT phenomenon about intangibles in national accounts, not a physical-capacity constraint — mapping it to robotics_lag is an analog (timing of realized/measured output), not a direct estimate of robot-building time. The 11.3%/15.9% TFP figures are cumulative level adjustments for IT/software over ~25 years, not AI-specific and not an annual passthrough rate. The 2.7-4.1x intangible ratio is a back-of-envelope bound (what WOULD be needed to explain the whole GDP slowdown), and the $12 market-value multiplier reflects equilibrium firm value, not marginal cash outlay — so the auto_cost mapping is an upper-leaning bound. The compute_effective_rate mapping is inferential (the paper says nothing about tax); it only supports the direction (low effective rate during expensed buildout). AI-specific effects were 'small but growing' with pre-2018 data — pre-ChatGPT, so timing presets should treat 2023, not 2017, as the AI J-curve's origin.

papers.title: Davis & von Wachter (2011), "Recessions and the Costs of Job Loss", Brookings Papers on Economic Activity (NBER WP 17638)
papers.verified: Fetched https://www.nber.org/papers/w17638 (abstract page) and the full working-paper PDF https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w17638/w17638.pdf (text extracted locally with pypdf); all quotes below are verbatim from those fetched texts.

key_numbers.quantity: PV earnings loss, displacement in tight labor market (U<6%), 20y horizon, 5% discount
key_numbers.value: 1.4 years of pre-displacement annual earnings
key_numbers.quote: In present value terms, men lose an average of 1.4 years of pre-displacement earnings if displaced in mass-layoff events that occur when the national unemployment rate is below 6 percent.

key_numbers.quantity: PV earnings loss, displacement in slack labor market (U>8%), 20y horizon, 5% discount
key_numbers.value: 2.8 years of pre-displacement annual earnings
key_numbers.quote: They lose a staggering 2.8 years of pre-displacement earnings if displaced when the unemployment rate exceeds 8 percent. These results reflect discounting at a 5% annual rate over 20 years after displacement.

key_numbers.quantity: Average-year PV reduction in future earnings (men, 3+ yrs tenure, mass layoff at larger firms)
key_numbers.value: 12 percent
key_numbers.quote: For men with 3 or more years of prior tenure who lose jobs in mass-layoff events at larger firms, job displacement reduces the present value of future earnings by 12 percent in an average year.

key_numbers.quantity: Year-1 earnings loss relative to control group
key_numbers.value: 40% (recession displacements) / 23% (expansion displacements)
key_numbers.quote: Earnings losses at displacement relative to the control group are very large initially, 40% in the first year after displacement for displacements that occur in recessions and 23% for displacements that occur in expansions.

key_numbers.quantity: Persistent earnings loss 10-20 years after displacement
key_numbers.value: ~20% (recession displacements) / ~10% (expansion displacements)
key_numbers.quote: The average earnings losses are about 20% from 10 to 20 years out for displacements that occur in recessions and about 10% for those that occur in expansions.

key_numbers.quantity: Cyclical amplification of PV losses
key_numbers.value: ~2x recession vs expansion
key_numbers.quote: Present value losses for displacements that occur in recessions are nearly twice as large as for displacements in expansions.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.11 (mild preset) to 0.22 (severe preset), central 0.15
lever_mappings.rationale: Convert PV losses to an annualized permanent earnings deficit with the paper's own frame (20y, 5%/yr): annuity factor = (1-1.05^-20)/0.05 = 12.46. Expansion displacement: 1.4/12.46 = 11.2%/yr; slack-market displacement: 2.8/12.46 = 22.5%/yr. A mass AI-displacement event resembles the slack case, so severe presets should sit in the 0.18-0.22 range. This matches the paper's independent 12% average-year PV statement.

lever_mappings.suggested: long-run floor 0.10-0.20 (never below 0.10 in any preset)
lever_mappings.rationale: The 10-20-years-out deficit (10% expansion / 20% recession) is nearly pure wage effect, since nonemployment spells have largely resolved by then. Because the model books unemployment separately via reabsorption_rate, the haircut lever should be anchored to this long-run component (0.10-0.20) rather than the year-1 40% figure, which bundles nonemployment time.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.15-0.35 /yr (slack-market presets)
lever_mappings.rationale: Recession-displaced deficit decays from 40% in year 1 to a ~20% permanent plateau by year ~10. If 20pp of the initial deficit is transitional (nonemployment plus temporary underemployment), eroding it over 8-10 years implies a full-recovery hazard of roughly 0.2-0.35/yr; expansion displacements (23%->10% faster) support the upper end in tight markets. Loose mapping - see caveats.

lever_mappings.suggested: validation target, not a direct value: pick demand_multiplier so a mass-displacement scenario roughly doubles per-worker PV earnings losses vs a marginal one
lever_mappings.rationale: The paper's key stylized fact is the 1.4x -> 2.8x doubling when displacement happens into a slack market. In the model, slack is endogenous (induced losses via demand_multiplier ~1 Okun-style with mpc 0.95 feeding lfp/reabsorption). A preset suite should reproduce approximately 2x per-worker losses between low-adoption and high-adoption paths; if it produces far more or less, the demand/survivor-elasticity block is miscalibrated.

papers.caveats: The PV losses are total earnings losses including nonemployment time, hours cuts, and foregone wage growth - mapping the full 1.4-2.8-year PV onto reemployment_haircut while also modeling unemployment spells via reabsorption_rate double-counts; use the long-run (10-20y) 10-20% component for the haircut and let the model generate the transitional part. Sample is men age <=50 with 3+ years tenure at firms with 50+ employees displaced in mass layoffs - higher-attachment and higher-loss than the average AI-exposed worker, and AI displacement of cognitive workers at healthy (not distressed) firms may differ. The recession/expansion split conditions on aggregate slack, which is endogenous in the model - do not apply the 2.8-year number AND a strong demand_multiplier simultaneously as exogenous inputs; use one to validate the other.

papers.title: Jacobson, LaLonde & Sullivan (1993), "Earnings Losses of Displaced Workers", American Economic Review 83(4), 685-709
papers.verified: Fetched https://ideas.repec.org/a/aea/aecrev/v83y1993i4p685-709.html and https://research.upjohn.org/up_workingpapers/11/ (both abstracts verified verbatim). The AER full text at princeton.edu is a scanned image PDF with no extractable text layer, so within-paper table figures could not be verified; one such number is flagged memory-only below.

key_numbers.quantity: Long-term annual earnings losses of high-tenure workers separating from distressed firms
key_numbers.value: 25 percent per year
key_numbers.quote: We find that when high-tenure workers separate from distressed firms their long-term losses average 25 percent per year.

key_numbers.quantity: Same headline result, published AER wording
key_numbers.value: 25 percent per year
key_numbers.quote: high-tenure workers separating from distressed firms suffer long-term losses averaging 25 percent per year

key_numbers.quantity: Pattern of losses: pre-separation dip, breadth across sectors, persistence within similar firms
key_numbers.value: losses begin before separation and persist even for those re-hired at similar firms
key_numbers.quote: Further, their losses mount even prior to separation, are not limited to workers in a few industrial sectors, and are substantial even for those who find new jobs in similar firms.

key_numbers.quantity: Quarterly dollar loss six years after separation (Pennsylvania admin data, mid-1980s dollars)
key_numbers.value: ~$1,600 per quarter, ~25% of pre-displacement earnings

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.25 as the pessimistic-preset ceiling (0.18-0.25 band for high-attachment cohorts)
lever_mappings.rationale: The 25%/yr long-term loss is measured 5-6 years after separation, when reemployment has largely stabilized, so it approximates a quasi-permanent earnings cut: haircut = 0.25 directly, no horizon conversion needed. Because the 25% is quarterly EARNINGS (includes reduced hours and residual nonemployment), the pure wage-rate haircut is somewhat lower - hence 0.18-0.25.

lever_mappings.suggested: keep haircut >= 0.15 even in presets with fast reabsorption_rate
lever_mappings.rationale: Losses are 'substantial even for those who find new jobs in similar firms' - i.e. the loss is not primarily an unemployment-duration effect. In the model this means the haircut lever, not reabsorption_rate, should carry most of the fiscal drag for displaced workers: raising reabsorption_rate should not be allowed to erase the earnings loss.

papers.caveats: This is the upper-bound estimate in the literature: Pennsylvania workers with 6+ years tenure separating from distressed firms during the severe early-1980s rust-belt recession - simultaneously high-tenure, mass-layoff, depressed-region, and manufacturing-heavy. AI displacement of shorter-tenure service/cognitive workers at profitable firms should be milder. The 25%/yr is an earnings concept (hours + wage + residual nonemployment), not a pure re-employment wage cut, so mapping it 1:1 onto reemployment_haircut while also modeling unemployment spells double-counts. The paper offers no reemployment hazard usable for reabsorption_rate, and the pre-separation earnings dip (losses 'mount even prior to separation') has no counterpart in the model, which shocks wages only at displacement.

papers.title: Farber (2015), "Job Loss in the Great Recession and its Aftermath: U.S. Evidence from the Displaced Workers Survey" (NBER WP 21216)
papers.verified: Fetched https://www.nber.org/papers/w21216 (abstract page), https://www.nber.org/digest/aug15/great-recession-job-losses-severe-enduring, and the full working-paper PDF https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w21216/w21216.pdf (text extracted locally with pypdf); quotes below are verbatim from the extracted PDF text (ligatures normalized).

key_numbers.quantity: Gross job-loss rate in the Great Recession (3-year window)
key_numbers.value: ~16% of workers (one in six) over 2007-2009, i.e. ~5.5%/yr
key_numbers.quote: These data show a record high rate of job loss in the Great Recession, with almost one in six workers reporting having lost a job in the 2007-2009 period, that has not yet returned to pre-recession levels.

key_numbers.quantity: Reemployment of full-time job losers, Jan 2010 survey
key_numbers.value: 52% employed; 80% of those full-time (~42% FT overall)
key_numbers.quote: Even among losers of full-time jobs between 2007 and 2009, only about 52 percent were employed in January 2010 and only 80 percent of those were reemployed in a full-time job.

key_numbers.quantity: Cumulative job-finding by elapsed time since loss (deep-slack market)
key_numbers.value: 44% within ~1 yr (2009 losers by Jan 2010); 69% within ~1.5-3 yrs (2007-08 losers)
key_numbers.quote: Again looking at job losses in the 2007-09 period, 69 percent of workers who lost jobs in 2007 or 2008 had held at least one job by January 2010. In contrast, only 44 percent of workers who lost jobs in 2009 had held at least one job by January 2010.

key_numbers.quantity: Average decline in real weekly earnings for reemployed job losers (raw, lost job vs survey-date job)
key_numbers.quote: the Great Recession is the largest in my sample period at 17.5 percent. This compares with a decline of 14.1 percent in 1984 and 15.9 percent in 1992. In contrast to the incomplete recovery in the aftermath of the Great Recession of the employment measures I examined, the average earnings decline of job losers was much less severe, with a decline of only 6.4 percent for jobs lost 2011-13.

key_numbers.quantity: Difference-in-difference FT-FT earnings loss including foregone counterfactual wage growth (1980s benchmark)
key_numbers.value: ~13% (= 9% own decline + 4.5% foregone control-group growth)
key_numbers.quote: The results show that in the 1980s displaced workers earned about 9 percent less on average after displacement than before while earnings for the control group rose by about 4.5 percent over the same period. The difference-in-difference estimate of the earnings loss is the difference between these numbers, which is a loss of about 13 percent during the 1980s.

key_numbers.quantity: Post-displacement labor-force exit share
key_numbers.value: ~10% of job losers NILF at survey date, stable across all cycles
key_numbers.quote: The survey-date fraction of job losers not in the labor force is remarkably constant across all years, at about 10 percent. There is no evidence that job losers are disproportionately discouraged in recessions, including the most recent recession, leading to withdrawal from the labor force.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.13 central (0.10-0.18 band) for moderate presets; use JLS/DvW 0.20-0.25 only for severe presets
lever_mappings.rationale: The counterfactual-adjusted FT-FT loss is ~13% (9% own + 4.5% foregone growth). The 17.5% Great Recession raw decline includes FT-to-PT hours cuts (PT share among reemployed FT losers hit 20% in 2010), which the model books partly through reabsorption to a service-floor wage; the pure wage-cut lever should sit near 0.13, rising toward 0.18 in slack presets.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.40-0.50 /yr in deep-slack (severe AI-shock) presets; 0.60-0.75 /yr in normal-market presets
lever_mappings.rationale: Cumulative job-finding: 44% of 2009 losers held a job within <=1 yr -> annual hazard >=0.44 (mean elapsed ~7 mo implies -ln(0.56)*(12/7) ~ 0.99 continuous, i.e. bounded above); 2007-08 losers with mean elapsed ~2 yrs give 1-(1-r)^2 = 0.69 -> r = 0.44/yr. So ~0.44/yr is a well-identified deep-recession hazard; pre-recession DWS employment rates (~70%+ at survey) support 0.6-0.75 in normal times.

10% of job losers are NILF at survey dates that average ~2 yrs after loss: 1-(1-x)^2 = 0.10 -> x = 0.051/yr. Since the model applies lfp_exit_rate only to benefit-exhausted workers (a subset), and the DWS 10% includes early retirees (overlap with attrition_rate), 0.03-0.05 is the defensible band; the striking stability across cycles argues against scaling it up in severe presets.

lever_mappings.suggested: carve 1-2 pp of the 10% NILF share out of lfp_exit_rate into attrition_rate
lever_mappings.rationale: Farber notes job losers aged 55-64 are 10+ pp more likely to exit the labor force, 'perhaps moving to early retirement' - i.e. part of the observed 10% NILF is retirement, which the model books under attrition_rate, not SSDI-bound lfp_exit_rate. Splitting ~10% NILF over ~2 yrs as ~0.035/yr SSDI-type exit + ~0.015/yr retirement keeps the two levers from double-counting.

lever_mappings.suggested: pacing check: annual displacement flow (feasibility x adoption increment) above ~5.5%/yr of the workforce exceeds the worst 3-year episode on record
lever_mappings.rationale: One-in-six job loss over 2007-09 = ~5.5%/yr gross displacement, the historical maximum, and it produced 52% reemployment and a 17.5% earnings decline. Presets whose adoption_path slope pushes AI displacement flows well past 5.5%/yr are outside all historical absorption experience, so reabsorption_rate and reemployment_haircut calibrated from this paper become extrapolations - flag such presets rather than forbidding them.

papers.caveats: DWS measures weekly earnings only for the reemployed, so the 13-17.5% declines are survivor-selected and understate average losses (Farber says so explicitly: the measure 'considers only those who are reemployed (50 percent of job losers in the Great Recession)'); it also has no long-horizon follow-up, so it cannot speak to the permanence that reemployment_haircut assumes - pair with JLS/DvW for persistence. 'Held at least one job by January 2010' overstates durable reemployment (employment AT the survey date was only ~50%), so the 0.44/yr hazard is an upper bound for reabsorption into stable work. The NILF 10% is a stock ~0-3 yrs after loss, not a hazard among benefit-exhausted workers, so the lfp_exit_rate conversion assumes exits concentrate in that group. Job loss in the DWS is involuntary layoff for any reason, not technology-specific displacement.

papers.title: Autor, Dorn, Hanson (2016), "The China Shock: Learning from Labor-Market Adjustment to Large Changes in Trade", Annual Review of Economics 8:205-240
papers.verified: Fetched and text-extracted full paper PDF: https://www.ddorn.net/papers/Autor-Dorn-Hanson-ChinaShock.pdf (all quotes verbatim from extracted text, ligatures normalized). Abstract cross-checked against https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-economics-080315-015041. One memory-only item (ADHS 2014 cumulative earnings ~46% of initial annual earnings) flagged in caveats, not used as a key number.

key_numbers.quantity: Duration of local labor-market adjustment (wages, LFP, unemployment)
key_numbers.value: >= 10 years (depressed wages/LFP, elevated unemployment for at least a full decade)
key_numbers.quote: Adjustment in local labor markets is remarkably slow, with wages and labor-force participation rates remaining depressed and unemployment rates remaining elevated for at least a full decade after the China trade shock commences.

key_numbers.quantity: Decomposition of a $1,000-per-worker import exposure shock into employment states (CZ level, 1990-2007)
key_numbers.value: mfg employment -0.60 pp, non-mfg -0.18 pp, unemployment +0.22 pp, NILF +0.55 pp of working-age population
key_numbers.quote: Specifically, a $1,000 increase in a CZ's per-worker import exposure reduces the fraction of the working age population employed in manufacturing and nonmanufacturing, respectively, by -0.60 and -0.18 percentage points (the latter of which is not significant), and raises the fraction of unemployed and out of the labor force by 0.22 and 0.55 percentage points.

key_numbers.quantity: Net reabsorption of displaced manufacturing workers at decade horizon (CZ level)
key_numbers.value: ~zero: employment-to-population falls at least one-for-one with the manufacturing decline
key_numbers.quote: Instead, overall CZ employment-to-population rates fall at least one-for-one with the decline in manufacturing employment, and generally by slightly more.

key_numbers.quantity: Transfer offset of trade-induced earnings losses (75th vs 25th percentile exposure CZ)
key_numbers.value: -$549/adult/yr wage income vs +$58/capita/yr transfers (~10% offset)
key_numbers.quote: Comparing again the residents of CZs at the 75th and 25th percentile of import exposure, those in the more exposed location experience a reduction in annual household wage and salary income per adult of $549, whereas per capita transfer income rises by approximately $58, thereby offsetting just a small portion of the earnings loss

key_numbers.quantity: Split of local earnings loss between job loss and wage decline per $1,000 exposure
key_numbers.value: $312/adult/yr from employment decline (0.78 pp on $40,000 earnings) + $213/adult/yr from a 0.76 log-point weekly wage decline among the ~70% employed
key_numbers.quote: Given average annual earnings of approximately $40,000 per worker, a reduction of employment by 0.78 percentage points (sum of columns 1 and 2) per $1,000 dollars of imports per worker lowers earnings per adult by approximately $312 per year (0.0078 x 40,000). The same shock reduces the average weekly wage by 0.76 log points among the approximately 70% of working-age adults who are employed, which implies an additional earnings loss per adult of $213 per year (0.0076 x 0.7 x 40,000).

key_numbers.quantity: Amplification of direct job losses via interindustry linkages and local demand (Acemoglu et al. 2016, as summarized in the review)
key_numbers.value: ~2x direct impact within manufacturing plus an equally large effect outside manufacturing (total ~3-4x first-round direct)
key_numbers.quote: Interindustry linkages thus magnify the employment effects of trade shocks, almost doubling the size of the impact within manufacturing and producing an equally large employment effect outside of manufacturing.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.05-0.10 /yr for a China-shock-calibrated preset (half-life ~7-14 years)
lever_mappings.rationale: Per $1,000 exposure, total employment falls 0.78 pp while unemployment+NILF rise 0.77 pp — i.e. ~99% of the net employment decline is still non-employment at horizons of 10-17 years (1990-2007 stacked differences), and e-pop falls at least one-for-one with the mfg decline. Under a constant reabsorption hazard r, the share still non-employed after T=10y is e^(-rT); even a generous reading (half the gap closed by year 10) gives r = ln(2)/10 ~ 0.07/yr, and ADH's near-total net persistence points lower. Since CZ net stocks conflate individual re-employment with induced losses among others, the worker-level hazard sits somewhat above the net figure: 0.05-0.10/yr.

NILF absorbs 0.55 pp of the 0.77 pp rise in non-employment, i.e. ~71% of the persistent displaced stock ends up out of the labor force at the decade horizon. In a competing-hazards setup where the displaced either reabsorb (r ~ 0.07/yr) or exit the labor force (x), a terminal NILF share of x/(x+r) = 0.71 implies x ~ 0.17/yr. The review confirms the disability channel at the worker level (exposed workers spend 'more years receiving Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI)') but NILF also includes early retirement and discouragement, so allocate only part of x to the SSDI-bound lever: 0.08-0.15/yr, with the residual in attrition_rate.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.03-0.05 /yr on top of demographic baseline
lever_mappings.rationale: The review reports 'Retirement benefits rise in more trade-exposed CZs, suggesting that adverse labor-market shocks induce more workers to retire earlier.' Assign the non-SSDI residual of the ~0.17/yr LFP-exit hazard (see lfp_exit_rate arithmetic) to early-retirement attrition: ~0.17 - (0.08 to 0.15) ~ 0.03-0.05/yr. Qualitative anchor only — the review gives no per-worker retirement hazard.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.15-0.30 permanent wage cut (or service-floor wage near the ~25th percentile state wage)
lever_mappings.rationale: The CZ-average wage falls 0.76 log points per $1,000 exposure across all ~70% employed ($213/adult/yr on a $40,000 base = 0.53% population-average), but this dilutes much larger cuts on the ~1.1% of working-age population actually displaced per $1,000; the review stresses losses are 'concentrated among workers in the bottom four wage deciles' and that bottom-tercile workers 'obtain lower earnings per year both while working at the initial firm and after relocating to new employers.' A 15-30% permanent haircut on the re-employed reconciles the small population-average with large per-mover losses, and the decade-plus wage depression supports treating it as permanent rather than transitory.

lever_mappings.suggested: >= 1.0 (induced losses at least equal to direct); consistent with a 1.0-1.5 setting
lever_mappings.rationale: Interindustry linkages 'almost doubl[e] the size of the impact within manufacturing' and produce 'an equally large employment effect outside of manufacturing' — i.e. total local job losses run ~3-4x the first-round direct effect, so induced/direct >= 1. ADH also find non-college non-mfg employment declines, 'suggesting the presence of negative local demand spillovers.' Cross-validates Chodorow-Reich's 1.8 cross-sectional multiplier from the demand side.

papers.caveats: Concept gaps: (1) the China shock hit geographically concentrated manufacturing (physical channel), whereas AI displacement is economy-wide and cognitive — national general-equilibrium offsets absent in the CZ design could be larger (or smaller) for AI, so decade-scale persistence is an analogy, not an estimate; (2) $1,000-per-worker import-exposure units do not translate into an automation share — only the ratios (NILF/unemployment split, transfer offset share, induced/direct) map cleanly; (3) CZ-level net stocks conflate individual reabsorption with induced job losses, so reabsorption_rate inferred from net persistence is a lower-bound-flavored calibration; (4) the +0.55 pp NILF response bundles SSDI, early retirement, and discouragement — the review quantifies no SSDI-specific hazard or ssdi_annual dollar amount (it notes only that SS retirement+disability and medical/income-assistance categories each grow >$15 per capita at the 75-25 gap, while TAA grows just $0.23); (5) the $58-per-capita transfer response (~$6 per $100 exposure) is a regional per-capita fiscal validation target, not a per-displaced-worker benefit; (6) memory-only (not in fetched text): ADHS (2014, QJE) report cumulative 1992-2007 earnings losses of roughly 46% of initial annual earnings for high- vs low-exposure workers — use only as a soft cross-check on reemployment_haircut.

papers.title: Chodorow-Reich (2019), "Geographic Cross-Sectional Fiscal Spending Multipliers: What Have We Learned?", AEJ: Economic Policy 11(2):1-34
papers.verified: Fetched published abstract page https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/pol.20160465 and full NBER working paper w23577 PDF (text-extracted): https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w23577/w23577.pdf; also https://www.nber.org/papers/w23577. All quotes verbatim from fetched text (WP wording where noted).

key_numbers.quantity: Preferred cross-sectional fiscal spending multiplier (output)
key_numbers.quote: my preferred point estimate for a cross-sectional multiplier is 1.8

key_numbers.quantity: Implied national multiplier with no monetary-policy offset (deficit-financed)
key_numbers.value: ~1.7 or above
key_numbers.quote: Applying the rough lower bound result, a cross-sectional multiplier of 1.8 implies a zero lower bound deficit-financed national multiplier of about 1.7 or above. (Published AEA version: 'the cross-sectional evidence suggests a national no-monetary-policy-response multiplier of 1.7 or above')

key_numbers.quantity: ARRA cost per job-year (author's own harmonized 2SLS estimates)
key_numbers.value: ~$50,000 (2009 dollars); cross-study range roughly $25K-$125K
key_numbers.quote: After applying a common econometric framework to instruments from each of the three studies, I consistently find a cost-per-job of the ARRA of roughly $50,000. [...] The cost-per-job across these studies ranges from roughly $25K to $125K, with around $50K emerging as a preferred number.

key_numbers.quantity: Employment effect of spending (job-years per $100K), cross-study distribution
key_numbers.value: ~2 job-years per $100K; range 0.76-3.93, mean 2.1, median 1.9
key_numbers.quote: Summing up, the estimate of 2 job-years per $100K in column (4) of table 1 appears broadly representative. Put on common footing, the ARRA studies find estimates in the range of 0.76 to 3.93, with a cross-study mean of 2.1 and median of 1.9.

key_numbers.quantity: Output-per-worker conversion factor and direct GSP output multiplier
key_numbers.value: Y/E = $105K (2009); direct GSP output multiplier estimate 1.76
key_numbers.quote: Output per worker Y/E in the national income accounts was $105K in 2009. Applying this number to the cost-per-job estimated in column (4) yields an implied output multiplier of around two, reassuringly close to the direct estimate of the output multiplier of 1.76 in column (5).

key_numbers.quantity: Multiplier on state spending changes driven by balanced-budget rules (Clemens & Miran 2012, survey table row; local financing, transitory)
key_numbers.value: on-impact multiplier 0.29, 95% CI [-0.22, 0.79]
key_numbers.quote: State balanced budget rules [...] "On-impact" multiplier of 0.29 [-0.22,0.79]

lever_mappings.suggested: 1.8 central; sensitivity band 1.0-2.5
lever_mappings.rationale: Concept match: the model has no monetary-policy reaction, and Chodorow-Reich shows the cross-sectional multiplier is 'a rough lower bound for a particular policy-relevant type of national multiplier, the closed economy, passive monetary policy, deficit-financed multiplier' — exactly the model's regime — with 1.8 cross-sectional implying >= ~1.7 nationally. Unit check against the model's Okun chain: 1.8 x $100K / $105K output-per-worker ~ 1.7 job-years per $100K of demand change, matching the directly estimated 2 job-years per $100K. Band 1.0-2.5 trims the cross-study employment range 0.76-3.93 (mean 2.1, median 1.9).

lever_mappings.suggested: Validation target: ~1 induced job-year lost per $50K (2009$) of net demand withdrawal, i.e. per ~$70-80K in mid-2020s dollars
lever_mappings.rationale: Cost-per-job ~$50,000 (range $25K-$125K; Wilson-respecification $57K = $100K/1.75) inverts to 2 job-years per $100K. After running a scenario, divide the model's induced (demand-channel) job losses into the net demand withdrawal: the ratio should land near $50K per job-year in 2009 dollars, inflated by the model's baseline_growth_rate/nominal wage path (~1.4-1.6x by 2026) to ~$70-80K. If mpc=0.95 and demand_multiplier=1.8 produce a materially different cost-per-job, the compensation-per-job denominator in the Okun conversion is miscalibrated.

lever_mappings.suggested: Apply the full demand_multiplier (~1.8) to state balanced-budget gap closure; treat 0.29x as a conservative low-end for the on-impact effect; the cut-vs-hike split itself is unidentified in this paper (keep default)
lever_mappings.rationale: The ARRA evidence most relevant to the state channel is windfall federal aid to states (FMAP/Medicaid match) relieving balanced-budget constraints, which generated ~2 job-years per $100K — symmetry implies state gap closure (the reverse experiment) destroys demand at the same rate, supporting running state cuts through the same demand_multiplier rather than a discounted one. The only direct balanced-budget-rule estimate surveyed (Clemens & Miran 2012) is an on-impact multiplier of 0.29 [-0.22, 0.79] with local financing — imprecise and CI spanning zero — so 0.29 serves as a low-end sensitivity, not the central case. The paper provides no estimate of how states split closure between spending cuts and rate hikes, so state_cut_share and state_rate_hike_cap remain free levers.

papers.caveats: Concept gaps: (1) all estimates come from spending expansions during 2009-2011 slack; applying them to AI-driven demand withdrawal assumes symmetry of contractions and expansions, which the paper does not test; (2) the 1.7+ national mapping assumes passive monetary policy — if the Fed eases in response to AI displacement, the effective national multiplier falls below the cross-sectional estimate, so 1.8 is an upper-regime calibration; (3) the paper notes higher multipliers under greater slack (state-dependence), so demand_multiplier arguably should rise endogenously as displacement accumulates — the model's constant lever cannot capture this; (4) dollar figures ($50K/job-year, $105K output/worker) are 2009 levels and must be inflated before comparison with model outputs; (5) the multiplier is total (direct + induced) output per dollar of spending, whereas the model's demand_multiplier converts net withdrawal into *induced* job losses — calibrate so the model's total response matches 1.8, not induced alone, to avoid double counting; (6) cross-sectional designs difference out federal financing and national confounds, making them cleanest for the state balanced-budget channel and only a lower bound for the federal/national demand channel.

papers.title: Guerreiro, Rebelo, Teles (2022) "Should Robots Be Taxed?" (Review of Economic Studies 89(1))
papers.verified: Fetched full text: https://www.kellogg.northwestern.edu/faculty/rebelo/htm/robots.pdf (December 2020 author manuscript of the REStud 2022 paper; abstract identical to published version). OUP page https://academic.oup.com/restud/article-abstract/89/1/279/6219962 was fetched but paywalled.

key_numbers.quantity: Optimal robot tax path (Mirrleesian plan, 2018 start): transitional, converging to zero
key_numbers.value: 5.1% / 2.2% / 0.6% ad-valorem on robot purchases in decades starting 2018/2028/2038; 0 thereafter
key_numbers.quote: The optimal robot tax is 5.1, 2.2, and 0.6 percent in the decades that start in 2018, 2028, and 2038, respectively. The robot tax is initially higher than the estimated effective tax rate of 1.8 percent in the status quo tax system after the 2017 tax reform. Once the initial generations retire, the optimal robot tax is zero.

key_numbers.quantity: Steady-state optimal robot tax
key_numbers.value: 0 (zero once current routine-worker generations retire)
key_numbers.quote: We find that it is optimal to tax robots while the current generations of routine workers, who can no longer move to non-routine occupations, are active in the labor force. Once these workers retire, optimal robot taxes are zero.

key_numbers.quantity: Status-quo US effective taxation of robots (equipment & software capital), pre/post-2017 TCJA
key_numbers.value: Effective capital-income tax 10% -> 5%; equivalent ad-valorem robot purchase tax 3.8% -> 1.8%
key_numbers.quote: ACEMOGLU et al. (2020) estimate that the effective tax rate on income from equipment and software capital declined from 10 to 5 percent after the 2017 tax reform. Assuming these tax rates apply to robots, they correspond to ad-valorem tax rates of 3.8 and 1.8 percent, respectively.

key_numbers.quantity: Aggregate welfare gain from the optimal robot tax (vs optimal income tax with robot tax constrained to zero)
key_numbers.value: 0.21% consumption-equivalent (utilitarian)
key_numbers.quote: In terms of utilitarian social welfare, the optimal robot tax leads to a consumption-equivalent gain of 0.21 percent.

key_numbers.quantity: Distribution of gains: initial old routine workers
key_numbers.value: Up to 2.2% of consumption (losses of initial non-routine workers of same order)
key_numbers.quote: The welfare gains of the initial old generations of routine workers are as high as 2.2 percent of consumption. The losses for the initial non-routine workers are of the same order of magnitude.

key_numbers.quantity: Counterfactual optimal plan starting 1988 (start of automation era) — upper bound on transitional tax
key_numbers.value: 9.1% / 2.9% / 0.7% in first three decades, then zero
key_numbers.quote: Optimal robot taxes are 9.1, 2.9, and 0.7 percent in the first, second, and third decades, respectively. The optimal robot tax is zero from 2018 on.

lever_mappings.suggested: GRT-transitional preset: 0.051*auto_cost in years 0-10 (~0.015 at auto_cost=0.3), 0.022*auto_cost years 10-20 (~0.007), 0.006*auto_cost years 20-30 (~0.002), 0 thereafter
lever_mappings.rationale: GRT's tau_x is ad-valorem on robot purchases; our lever is a share of the saved compensation bill. Annual automation input spend in our model = auto_cost * saved_bill, so revenue-equivalent lever value = tau_x * auto_cost. E.g. 5.1% * 0.3 = 0.0153. The declining decade profile can be approximated as a linear decay to zero over 30 years.

lever_mappings.suggested: GRT-steady-state preset: 0
lever_mappings.rationale: Headline result: once workers who chose occupations before the automation shock retire, the optimal robot tax is exactly zero. A 'long-run optimal' preset should turn the lever off.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.05 (post-2017 status quo); 0.10 for a pre-TCJA counterfactual
lever_mappings.rationale: Direct concept match: the Acemoglu et al. (2020) effective tax rate on income from equipment and software capital that GRT use to calibrate the status quo is exactly an effective tax rate on the automation-capital pool. Their conversion formula tau_x = [r/(1-tau)+delta]/(r+delta)-1 links this rate to the ad-valorem equivalents (10%->3.8%, 5%->1.8%).

papers.caveats: Quotes are from the December 2020 author manuscript (Kellogg), not the paginated REStud version. Base mismatch: their robot tax is ad-valorem on robot purchases, so the mapping onto automation_tax_rate (share of saved wage bill) depends multiplicatively on auto_cost. Their 'robots' means ALL routine-biased technical progress, so the concept spans both our cognitive and physical channels, but the 10-year model periods and OLG retirement structure do not map onto annual reabsorption_rate/attrition_rate. The optimal-tax rationale (loosening incentive constraints while old routine workers cannot re-skill) relies on endogenous occupational choice of future generations — a margin our model lacks entirely; in our model the tax is purely a fiscal transfer with no efficiency role. Their welfare-optimal rates are not revenue-maximizing rates.

papers.title: Thuemmel (2023) "Optimal Taxation of Robots" (Journal of the European Economic Association 21(3), 1154-1190)
papers.verified: Fetched full text of the CESifo WP 7317 version: https://www.ifo.de/DocDL/cesifo1_wp7317.pdf (October 2018). Published JEEA abstract excerpts verified via https://econpapers.repec.org/article/oupjeurec/v_3a21_3ay_3a2023_3ai_3a3_3ap_3a1154-1190..htm. The OUP full text (academic.oup.com/jeea/article/21/3/1154/6798383) was paywalled; published-version point estimates not independently verified.

key_numbers.quantity: Optimal robot tax for the US (WP version, calibrated to Acemoglu-Restrepo wage impacts)
key_numbers.value: 1.8% with fixed occupations; 0.86% with occupational choice (ad-valorem on robots)
key_numbers.quote: Quantitatively, in the US, the optimal robot tax equals 1.8% if occupations are fixed, but decreases to 0.86% with occupational choice. The welfare impact of introducing a robot tax is small.

key_numbers.quantity: Welfare gain of introducing the robot tax
key_numbers.value: $21.14 per person per year (fixed occupations); $9.22 (occupational switching)
key_numbers.quote: Without occupational switching, the optimal robot tax is 1.8%. The welfare gain of introducing a robot tax expressed in dollars per person per year is 21.14$. With occupational switching, the robot tax equals 0.86%, and its welfare impact is reduced to 9.22$.

key_numbers.quantity: Upper bound on welfare gain as robot prices fall
key_numbers.value: Never exceeds $25 per person per year; eventually negligible
key_numbers.quote: Without occupational switching, the welfare gain from taxing robots increases at first as the price of robots falls, but it never exceeds 25$ per person per year. Moreover, in both scenarios the welfare gain of taxing robots eventually becomes negligible.

key_numbers.quantity: Published JEEA version: sign of optimal robot tax depends on robot price
key_numbers.value: Subsidy when robots expensive -> tax as robots get cheaper
key_numbers.quote: When robots are expensive, a robot subsidy is optimal. As robots get cheaper, it becomes optimal to tax them.

key_numbers.quantity: Published JEEA version: value of differentiating robot tax from general equipment-capital tax
key_numbers.value: ~0 (income-tax adjustment captures almost all gains)
key_numbers.quote: When reforming the status-quo tax system, most welfare gains can be achieved by adjusting the income tax. The additional gains from taxing robots differently from other equipment capital are close to zero.

lever_mappings.suggested: Thuemmel preset: 0.0086*auto_cost to 0.018*auto_cost (~0.003-0.005 at auto_cost=0.3)
lever_mappings.rationale: His tax is ad-valorem on robots; lever value = tau * auto_cost (annual robot spend = auto_cost * saved_bill). Use the 1.8% end when reabsorption_rate is ~0 (his fixed-occupations case) and the 0.86% end when reabsorption_rate is high (his occupational-choice case halves the optimum).

lever_mappings.suggested: Thuemmel-published preset: ~0, with compute_effective_rate at the economy-wide equipment-capital rate (~0.05)
lever_mappings.rationale: The published version's headline is that taxing robots DIFFERENTLY from other equipment capital adds close to zero welfare; the literature-faithful preset is no robot surtax, only the general capital tax carried by compute_effective_rate (GRT's cited post-2017 effective equipment rate is 5%).

lever_mappings.suggested: Pairing rule: automation_tax_rate at the high (1.8%-equivalent) end only when reabsorption_rate <= ~0.05/yr; use the 0.86%-equivalent when reabsorption is active
