lever_mappings.rationale: Occupational switching is the closest analogue of our reabsorption channel; in his model it cuts the optimal robot tax from 1.8% to 0.86% and the welfare gain from $21.14 to $9.22/person/yr, so presets should couple the two levers.

papers.caveats: The verified point estimates (1.8%/0.86%, $21.14/$9.22, <$25) come from the 2018 CESifo working-paper version; the published JEEA 2023 version adds other capital and reframes the result (subsidy when robots expensive, near-zero gain from differential taxation) — its abstract sentences are verified but its point estimates were behind the paywall. His optimum can be NEGATIVE (a subsidy); our automation_tax_rate is bounded at 0 and cannot express that. The wage-compression mechanism (robot tax compresses the wage distribution to relax income-tax incentive constraints) has no counterpart in our kernel — our tax is a pure revenue transfer from retained profit. Welfare gains in $/person/yr are welfare metrics, not revenue, and should not be read as fiscal yields.

papers.title: Costinot, Werning (2023) "Robots, Trade, and Luddism: A Sufficient Statistic Approach to Optimal Technology Regulation" (Review of Economic Studies 90(5))
papers.verified: Fetched full text of NBER WP 25103 (September 2018): https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w25103/w25103.pdf, plus the NBER abstract page https://www.nber.org/papers/w25103. Published REStud version (academic.oup.com/restud/article-abstract/90/5/2261/6798670) paywalled; WP numbers match the range cited in the published abstract per secondary sources.

key_numbers.quantity: Headline sufficient-statistic optimal robot tax for the US
key_numbers.value: 1% to 3.7% (ad-valorem on robot spending)
key_numbers.quote: Using the reduced-form evidence of Acemoglu and Restrepo (2017b) on the impact of robots in the United States, we find efficient taxes on robots ranging from 1% to 3.7%.

key_numbers.quantity: Dependence on the labor-supply elasticity used in the formula
key_numbers.quote: For a labor supply elasticity of ǫ = 0.1, in the low range of the micro-estimates reviewed in Chetty (2012), equation (3) leads to an optimal tax on robots equal to t∗m = 1%. For higher labor supply elasticities of ǫ = 0.3 or ǫ = 0.5, the previous tax goes up to t∗m = 2.7% and t∗m = 3.7%, respectively.

key_numbers.quantity: Comparative static: direction of the optimal tax as automation deepens
key_numbers.value: Magnitude may DECREASE as automation deepens and inequality rises
key_numbers.quote: while distributional concerns create a rationale for non-zero taxes on robots and trade, the magnitude of these taxes may decrease as the process of automation and globalization deepens and inequality increases.

key_numbers.quantity: Size of the US robot tax base used in the calibration
key_numbers.value: Labor earnings / robot spending ~ 245 (robots ~0.4% of the wage bill); robots ~0.64% of capital services
key_numbers.quote: the ratio of total labor earnings to total Chinese imports in 2007 is only 26.4, an order of magnitude smaller than the ratio to total spending on robots, around 245. [footnote:] Graetz and Michaels (2018) estimate the share of robots in total capital services to be around 0.64%.

key_numbers.quantity: Wage-incidence sufficient statistic behind the tax
key_numbers.value: Relative wage elasticity to robot quantity ~0.5 (Acemoglu-Restrepo based)
key_numbers.quote: the average value of the relative wage elasticity, d ln ω d ln y∗m, is of the same order of magnitude as the one implied by the estimates of Acemoglu and Restrepo (2017b), around 0.5.

key_numbers.quantity: Contrast case: efficient tariffs on Chinese imports (same formula, bigger base)
key_numbers.quote: This leads to an optimal tax on Chinese imports that is much smaller than the tax on robots: t∗m = 0.03% for ǫ = 0.1, 0.08% for ǫ = 0.3, and 0.11% for ǫ = 0.5.

lever_mappings.suggested: CW sufficient-statistic preset: 0.01*auto_cost to 0.037*auto_cost, central 0.027*auto_cost (~0.003-0.011, central ~0.008 at auto_cost=0.3)
lever_mappings.rationale: Their t* is ad-valorem on robot spending; lever = t* * auto_cost since our annual automation spend = auto_cost * saved_bill. The 1%/2.7%/3.7% ladder is indexed by the labor-supply elasticity (0.1/0.3/0.5), giving a natural low/central/high preset triple.

lever_mappings.suggested: Path rule: hold constant or ramp DOWN as cumulative adoption_path rises; never scale up with automation depth
lever_mappings.rationale: Their comparative static shows the optimal tax magnitude may fall as automation deepens and inequality increases — the opposite of the intuitive 'tax more as robots spread' rule; matches GRT's declining transitional path.

lever_mappings.suggested: Negative sign (substitution) for physical-channel presets; magnitude order ~0.5 relative-wage elasticity
lever_mappings.rationale: The sufficient statistic is the Acemoglu-Restrepo semi-elasticity of wages to robot penetration, negative through most of the wage distribution — qualitative support for a substitution-signed survivor_elasticity, though their elasticity is w.r.t. robot quantity, not labor-market slack, so use sign and order of magnitude only.

papers.caveats: Same base-conversion caveat: t* applies to the buyer price of robots, so mapping to automation_tax_rate scales with auto_cost. Their calibration is 2000s-era industrial robots — a base of only ~0.4% of labor earnings; the optimal PERCENT may transplant to AI-scale automation but the implied revenue does not, and nothing in the paper covers the cognitive channel. The formula is static (no transition dynamics), so there is no mapping to robotics_lag or adoption_path timing. The 'tax falls as automation deepens' result is from a stylized Rawlsian example, not a calibrated US path. Quotes are from the 2018 NBER WP; the REStud 2023 published text may differ in wording (the 1%-3.7% range is retained in the published abstract per secondary sources).

papers.title: Holtmann, Braun, Cho, Koch, Langenmayr (2025) "Investment Effects of a Quasi-Robot Tax: Evidence from South Korea" (arqus DP No. 308)
papers.verified: Fetched full text from EconStor: https://www.econstor.eu/bitstream/10419/335022/1/1948706962.pdf (arqus Discussion Paper 308, November 2025). Abstract cross-checked on https://ideas.repec.org/p/zbw/arqudp/335022.html. SSRN page returned 403.

key_numbers.quantity: Size of the quasi-robot tax: 2018 cut in Korea's automation investment tax credit
key_numbers.value: Medium firms 5% -> 3%, large firms 3% -> 1% of acquisition cost (a 2 p.p. rise in the net price of automation assets)
key_numbers.quote: Until 2017, medium-sized entities qualified for a 5% credit and large entities for a 3% credit. From 2018 onward, these rates were reduced to 3% and 1%, respectively (see Panel A of Table 1).

key_numbers.quantity: Robot-installation response (cross-country DiD vs Japan)
key_numbers.value: -28% robot installations after the reform
key_numbers.quote: We find that Korean industries decreased robot installations by 28% relative to their Japanese counterparts following the reform.

key_numbers.quantity: Firm-level investment response of treated firms
key_numbers.value: ~ -3 percentage points of total assets (tangible + intangible fixed investment)
key_numbers.quote: On average, treated firms reduced their investment in tangible and intangible fixed assets (relative to total assets) by approximately 3%-points relative to unaffected firms.

key_numbers.quantity: Employment response (robots-replace-workers evidence)
key_numbers.value: Positive for financially unconstrained firms; full-sample average ~0
key_numbers.quote: We find evidence of a positive effect on firm-level employment in the sub-sample of less financially constrained firms, suggesting that robots and employees act as substitutes here; the average effect in the full sample is, however, statistically indistinguishable from zero.

key_numbers.quantity: Concurrent estimate (Kang et al. 2024) of the employment effect of the same reform
key_numbers.value: +3.8% employment for firms with revenue 50-200bn KRW; earnings per worker fall
key_numbers.quote: For the subgroup of firms with revenues between 50 and 200 billion Korean Won, they find that employment increases by about 3.8%, while earnings per worker decrease.

key_numbers.quantity: Materiality of the credit to firms (pre-reform)
key_numbers.value: 5.2%-15% of total tax payable among users; ~$430m claimed in manufacturing in 2019
key_numbers.quote: Among users, the benefit corresponded to 5.2% to 15% of total tax payable, depending on firm size. Thus, the tax credit for automation investment is substantial, making it plausible that its reduction affects investment behavior.

lever_mappings.suggested: BEHAVIORAL FLAG, not a level: any preset with ad-valorem-equivalent (automation_tax_rate / auto_cost) above ~0.02 should be treated as adoption-suppressing
lever_mappings.rationale: Korea's ~2 p.p. rise in the effective price of automation assets cut robot installations 28% — an arc semi-elasticity of roughly -14 per unit of ad-valorem tax. Our model's automation_tax_rate has NO investment response (pure revenue transfer from retained profit), so it mechanically overstates both revenue and displacement at literature-scale rates unless the response is hand-coded via other levers.

lever_mappings.suggested: Hand-coded response: scale the physical-channel adoption ceiling (end value) down by ~14 x (automation_tax_rate / auto_cost), i.e. ~28% lower per 2 p.p. ad-valorem tax, capped at ~50%
lever_mappings.rationale: The 28% installation decline per 2 p.p. price increase is the only quasi-experimental robot-tax investment elasticity available; applying it to the adoption ceiling emulates the missing behavioral margin in robot-tax presets.

lever_mappings.suggested: Alternative hand-coding: lengthen robotics_lag by ~25-30% under robot-tax presets instead of (not in addition to) cutting the adoption ceiling
lever_mappings.rationale: The measured outcome is a flow (annual installations), so a slower capacity ramp is an equally faithful reading of the -28% flow response as a lower long-run ceiling; choose one encoding to avoid double-counting.

lever_mappings.suggested: Sign check only: substitution (negative) in the physical channel
lever_mappings.rationale: Making robots dearer raised employment at unconstrained firms (+3.8% in the Kang et al. subgroup) with earnings per worker falling — direct evidence that robots and workers are substitutes at the margin, and that reabsorbed labor comes in at lower wages (qualitatively consistent with a positive reemployment_haircut).

papers.caveats: This is the paper whose core finding our model cannot represent: an investment/adoption behavioral response to the tax price of automation — automation_tax_rate in our kernel changes only the fiscal split, never adoption. The 'tax' is actually a tax-credit reduction (2 p.p. of acquisition cost), not a levy on the saved wage bill; converting it to our lever requires the auto_cost bridge and assumes credit cuts and taxes are symmetric. External validity is weak for US AI scenarios: Korea is the world's densest robot adopter (1,000 robots per 10,000 manufacturing employees), the evidence covers industrial robots only (physical channel — says nothing about cognitive_feasibility or the cognitive channel), and treatment turns partly on Korean business-group membership. The firm-level outcome is total tangible+intangible investment (a proxy, not robots). Effects are heterogeneous by financial constraint — a representative-firm disposition split (retained_profit/price/survivor shares) cannot express that constrained firms cut investment while unconstrained firms substituted toward labor. Working paper, not yet peer-reviewed.

papers.title: Korinek & Suh (2024), "Scenarios for the Transition to AGI", NBER WP 32255
papers.verified: Fetched https://www.nber.org/papers/w32255 (abstract) and the full 46-page PDF https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w32255/w32255.pdf (text-extracted locally). All quotes verified against the extracted full text; fi/ff ligatures dropped by PDF extraction were restored (e.g. 'reects'->'reflects').

key_numbers.quantity: Business-as-usual task automation rate (fraction of remaining tasks automated per year)
key_numbers.value: lambda*g = 0.01 per year
key_numbers.quote: scenario business-as-usual, in which Φ(i) reflects a rate of task automation of λg = 0.01 per year

key_numbers.quantity: Business-as-usual output growth (validation target for baseline_growth_rate, real)
key_numbers.quote: We observe that in the business-as-usual scenario, output grows at approximately 2% per year, and both the returns to capital and the wage bill rise approximately in tandem (with a small decline in the labor share due to the effects of automation).

key_numbers.quantity: AGI scenario horizons: time to full automation of all tasks
key_numbers.value: 20 years (baseline AGI) / 5 years (aggressive AGI)
key_numbers.quote: Panels (b) and (c) show the AGI scenarios, in which the fraction of unautomated tasks collapses to zero in 20 or 5 years, respectively.

key_numbers.quantity: Timing of wage collapse in the aggressive (AGI-in-5y) scenario
key_numbers.value: ~3 years after start (i.e. before full automation)
key_numbers.quote: In the aggressive AGI scenario, the wage collapse happens after about 3 years.

key_numbers.quantity: Post-collapse steady-state output growth under full automation (vs 2% BAU; intro states 'ten times faster growth than in the business-as-usual scenario')
key_numbers.quote: After the collapse, wages are equal to the returns to capital, and the economy remains in region 2 where labor and capital are perfectly substitutable, with steady-state growth of 18% per year.

key_numbers.quantity: Mixed (bout-of-cognitive-automation) scenario: years of wage collapse before capital accumulation restores labor demand
key_numbers.value: ~9 years
key_numbers.quote: Around year 9, the economy has accumulated sufficient capital so that it returns to region 1. Wages rise above A and start growing again in line with further (slower) advances in automation and further capital accumulation.

lever_mappings.suggested: Three preset values: BAU 0 -> 0.18-0.26 over 20-30y; AGI-20y 0 -> 1.0 with n_periods=20; AGI-5y 0 -> 1.0 with n_periods=5 (front-loaded: >~2/3 of the pool automated by year 3)
lever_mappings.rationale: BAU: unautomated tasks decay exponentially at lambda*g=0.01/yr, so cumulative share automated by year t = 1-e^(-0.01t) = 0.18 at t=20, 0.26 at t=30. Baseline AGI: unautomated fraction hits zero at t=20 -> adoption ceiling reaches 1.0 in 20 periods. Aggressive AGI: zero at t=5; wages collapse at ~3y, implying most of the pool is automated by then. Note the paper's automation index conflates feasibility and adoption, so these paths assume feasibility levers are set near 1 in the AGI presets.

lever_mappings.suggested: Mixed preset: ~1.0 for cognitive with fast adoption, while physical_feasibility stays well below 1 (long tail of hard tasks); AGI presets: both feasibilities -> 1.0
lever_mappings.rationale: The fourth scenario is 'a large bout of automation in the near term—for example because AI rapidly automates cognitive jobs—but ... there remains a long tail of tasks that are harder to automate.' That is exactly the model's cognitive/physical split: near-total cognitive feasibility now, bounded physical/long-tail feasibility. The AGI-defined scenarios (all tasks automatable) mean both feasibility levers at 1.0.

lever_mappings.suggested: BAU preset: 0 to +0.2 (mild complementarity); AGI-20y and AGI-5y presets: strongly negative, -0.5 to -1.0
lever_mappings.rationale: Sign/regime anchor, not an econometric estimate. In BAU the wage bill rises in tandem with output (wages grow ~2%/yr with labor scarce -> weak complementarity). In the AGI scenarios labor becomes perfectly substitutable with capital once automation outpaces capital accumulation and wages collapse to the machine-equivalent rate ('wages are equal to the returns to capital') -> substitution sign, magnitude large enough that rising slack drives survivor wages down toward the machine cost floor. Also implies survivor_raise_ceiling ~1.0 (no raises) in AGI presets.

lever_mappings.suggested: AGI presets: ~1.0 (lever maximum); BAU preset: 0.3-0.5
lever_mappings.rationale: Full automation lifts growth from ~2%/yr to 18%/yr ('ten times faster growth'). With a 66% initial labor share (calibration: Phi0=0.608, K0=4.6 chosen 'to match a 66% initial labor share'), the cumulative output gain from automating the labor bill far exceeds the saved wage bill itself, so the GDP dividend lever should sit at its upper bound in AGI presets. In BAU, most of the 2% trend belongs in baseline_growth_rate, leaving a modest incremental dividend.

lever_mappings.suggested: Mixed preset: ~0.1/yr (recovery over ~9 years); AGI presets: ~0 (no recovery, wages stay collapsed)
lever_mappings.rationale: In the mixed scenario labor demand collapses, then recovers 'around year 9' once capital accumulation restores labor scarcity -> annualized re-employment/recovery hazard ~1/9 = 0.11/yr. In the bounded-task AGI scenarios the economy stays in region 2 forever, so displaced labor demand never returns -> set reabsorption near zero in those presets.

papers.caveats: This is a full-employment general-equilibrium model: wages adjust and no one is unemployed, so 'wage collapse' maps to the model's wage levers (survivor_elasticity, reemployment_haircut, survivor_raise_ceiling) rather than to displacement headcounts; unemployment-side levers (ui_weeks, lfp_exit_rate, attrition_rate) have no counterpart. Tasks are measured in compute-complexity space, not compensation dollars, so lambda*g=0.01/yr is not directly a share of the wage bill. The 18%/yr post-AGI growth comes from endogenous AK-style capital accumulation, which exceeds anything a one-shot productivity_passthrough in [0,1] can produce. There is no firm-disposition, price, tax, or fiscal channel at all, and no adoption friction distinct from feasibility (the automation index is both). The year-9 recovery in the mixed scenario is driven by capital deepening restoring labor scarcity, not by displaced workers taking lower-wage service jobs, so the reabsorption_rate mapping is a mechanism analogy.

papers.title: Korinek & Stiglitz (2019), "Artificial Intelligence and Its Implications for Income Distribution and Unemployment", NBER WP 24174 / chapter in The Economics of Artificial Intelligence: An Agenda
papers.verified: Fetched https://www.nber.org/papers/w24174 (abstract) and the full 45-page PDF https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w24174/w24174.pdf (text-extracted locally). All quotes verified against the extracted full text. Note: the paper is a theory taxonomy with almost no calibrated magnitudes.

key_numbers.quantity: Share of US jobs at risk of automation (cited from Frey & Osborne 2017; feasibility ceiling, cognitive+physical combined)
key_numbers.quote: For example, Frey and Osborne (2017) warn that 47% of jobs in the US economy are at risk of being automated by advances in AI‐related fields.

key_numbers.quantity: Expert AGI timing (cited from Bostrom 2014; anchors which preset family is 'central')
key_numbers.value: median ~2040-2050; 90% probability within the century
key_numbers.quote: although the median estimate in the AI expert community is around 2040 to 2050, with most AI experts assigning a 90% probability to human‐ level general artificial intelligence arising within the current century (see Bostrom, 2014)

key_numbers.quantity: Efficiency/subsistence wage floor mechanism (wage floor below which displacement becomes unemployment, not wage cuts)
key_numbers.value: qualitative (wages cannot fall below cost of living)
key_numbers.quote: If technological progress continues unabated and the marginal product of workers declines below their cost of living, then classic nutritional efficiency wage theories apply: unemployment would result because workers could not survive working for the market‐clearing wage without government support.

key_numbers.quantity: Frictionless singularity benchmark: wage effect of machine labor when capital adjusts (survivor_elasticity = 0 case), plus transitional decline when it does not
key_numbers.value: wage unchanged if K grows with H+M; labor demand falls temporarily otherwise
key_numbers.quote: the human wage may actually be unchanged because capital K grows in proportion to effective labor (H + M) so that the marginal productivity of labor and the wage remain unchanged. ... When factors are slow to adjust, the pattern of transition can be complex, with demand for human labor typically going down temporarily.

key_numbers.quantity: Non-distorting ex-post windfall tax illustration (rent taxation of the innovators' surplus)
key_numbers.value: 50% of returns over $10 billion with no incentive effect
key_numbers.quote: If Bill Gates had been told, ex ante, that government would take away 50% of his returns over $10 billion, there is little reason to believe that it would have had any significant effect on innovation and investment.

key_numbers.quantity: Feasibility of compensating losers even under a singularity (justifies redistribution levers existing at all)
key_numbers.value: qualitative (non-distortionary compensation possible)
key_numbers.quote: Under plausible conditions, non-distortionary taxation can be levied to compensate those who otherwise might lose.

lever_mappings.suggested: ~0.4-0.5 combined feasible share across cognitive+physical (e.g. cognitive_feasibility 0.5 with physical_feasibility 0.4) for a 'Frey-Osborne' central preset
lever_mappings.rationale: The paper's quantitative anchor for exposure is the cited Frey-Osborne 47% of US jobs at risk. That is a technical-feasibility ceiling over a decade or two, not an adoption rate, so it belongs in the feasibility levers with adoption_path handling diffusion; weight the split so employment-weighted total feasible work ~0.47.

lever_mappings.suggested: Implement as the service-floor variant: floor the re-employment wage at subsistence (a low state wage percentile, e.g. 10th-20th), and route workers whose implied market wage falls below the floor into unemployment/lfp_exit_rate instead of deeper cuts
lever_mappings.rationale: The efficiency-wage channel says wages cannot adjust below the cost of living even in the long run; displacement past that point produces unemployment needing 'government support' rather than ever-lower wages. So the haircut should be bounded by a floor, with the residual absorbed by lfp_exit_rate > 0 (SSDI) in deep-automation presets — exactly the paper's 'government support' margin.

lever_mappings.suggested: Keep finite and >0 as the binding transition friction; no point estimate in the paper — treat as a scenario driver (slower reabsorption = larger transitional unemployment)
lever_mappings.rationale: The paper's second unemployment channel is definitionally the model's transition pool: 'technological unemployment arises as a transition phenomenon, when jobs are replaced at a faster rate than workers can find new ones.' It validates the model structure (displacement rate vs reabsorption hazard race) but supplies no calibrated hazard.

lever_mappings.suggested: >0 defensible without efficiency loss to the extent it falls on the innovators' surplus/rents: modest 0.05-0.15 on the full saved bill, or up to ~0.5 if targeted at the rent-like retained_profit_share component
lever_mappings.rationale: Abstract: non-distortionary taxation can compensate losers; the 50%-of-returns-over-$10B example says high ex-post rates on winner-take-all rents have little incentive cost. The model's automation_tax_rate is levied on the saved compensation bill and paid out of retained profit, so it approximates the paper's surplus tax only insofar as retained_profit_share captures rents rather than normal returns.

lever_mappings.suggested: Long-run/BAU presets: ~0 (frictionless capital adjustment leaves wages unchanged); transition presets: negative, ~-0.2 to -0.5
lever_mappings.rationale: The singularity benchmark has wages unchanged when capital grows in proportion to effective labor (H+M) -> elasticity 0; with slow factor adjustment 'demand for human labor typically going down temporarily' -> negative sign during the transition, magnitude unquantified. Supports making survivor_elasticity more negative in faster-adoption presets.

papers.caveats: This is a taxonomy/theory paper: apart from cited figures (Frey-Osborne 47%, Bostrom timing), it contains no calibrated magnitudes, so every mapping is a sign/structure anchor rather than a point estimate. The 47% is jobs-at-risk (feasibility), not tasks or wage-bill shares, and is the authors quoting others. The efficiency-wage floor is absolute subsistence, not a statutory or state-percentile wage, so the service-floor mapping is an approximation. Pareto-improvement and non-distortionary-taxation results assume lump-sum instruments and ex-ante contracting that the fiscal model (bracket-based income/payroll taxes, balanced-budget states) does not have. The paper also stresses cases where redistribution is infeasible and technology should be steered instead — a channel with no lever in the model.

papers.title: Korinek & Lockwood (2026), "Public Finance in the Age of AI: A Primer", NBER WP 34873
papers.verified: Fetched https://www.nber.org/papers/w34873 (abstract page; the NBER PDF itself returned HTTP 403). Full text verified via the Brookings working-paper version (dated December 30, 2025, posted January 2026, 53 pp.): https://www.brookings.edu/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/Korinek-Lockwood-FINAL-for-website.pdf — abstract matches the NBER page. All quotes are from the Brookings full text.

key_numbers.quantity: Optimal tax rate on AGI capital (Stage 2 'optimal harvesting' result)
key_numbers.value: = human discount rate, ~4%/yr of the AGI capital stock
key_numbers.quote: In a simple specification of the problem, we find that the optimal tax rate on AGI capital equals the human discount rate—commonly estimated around 4 percent—reflecting the fundamental tradeoff between current human consumption and future growth.

key_numbers.quantity: Tax rate on identifiable AGI rents (market concentration / fixed factors)
key_numbers.value: up to ~100% without distortion
key_numbers.quote: To the extent that such rents can be identified and isolated, they could be taxed at rates approaching 100 percent without distorting the AGI’s investment decisions—rents are by definition not necessary to induce the activity generating them.

key_numbers.quantity: US macro tax benchmarks: tax-to-GDP ratio and single-instrument equivalents (validation targets for the revenue ledger)
key_numbers.value: tax/GDP ~0.25; ~31% pure income-tax equivalent; ~37.5% pure consumption-tax equivalent (consumption ~2/3 of income)
key_numbers.quote: the U.S. macroeconomic tax rate, or tax-to-GDP ratio, is around 0.25 (OECD 2024). ... that would correspond to a tax rate of about 31% (= 0.25 / 0.8). If instead all tax revenue were raised from a proportional consumption tax, then, given that consumption is about two-thirds of income (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis 2025, NIPA Table 1.1.10), that would correspond to a consumption tax rate of about 37.5% (= 0.25 / (2/3)).

key_numbers.quantity: Calibrated optimal capital-income tax in the iso-elastic benchmark (Stage 1-2 boundary)
key_numbers.value: tau*_K ~ 11% of net capital income (beta=.96, sigma=.5, net capital productivity 20%)
key_numbers.quote: For example, for the standard values β = .96 and σ = .5 and for net capital productivity of 20% so γA = 1.2 we find that τ ⋆ K ≈ 11%.

key_numbers.quantity: Labor tax base erosion limit (validation target: income+payroll revenue must -> 0 as displacement -> 1)
key_numbers.value: max labor-tax revenue / output -> 0 as capital share -> 1
key_numbers.quote: We show formally that maximum labor-tax revenue as a share of output approaches zero as the capital share approaches one.

key_numbers.quantity: Staging of robot/compute taxes (when automation_tax_rate and compute_effective_rate should switch on)
key_numbers.value: Stage 1: ~0 (distortionary); Stage 2 (AGI economy): the appropriate instruments
key_numbers.quote: Proposals that tax AI-related capital goods (e.g., compute taxes and robot taxes) would distort investment during Stage 1 and are more appropriate for Stage 2 when accessing AGI’s value creation requires taxing capital directly.

lever_mappings.suggested: AGI-stage presets: 0.04 (range 0.02-0.11 depending on base); transition/Stage-1 presets: ~0
lever_mappings.rationale: The optimal harvesting tax on AGI capital is ~4%/yr of the capital stock — if the model's compute_effective_rate applies annually to the compute-capital pool value, 0.04 is a direct anchor. If instead it is read as a tax on capital income: tau*_K ~ 11% of a 20% net return = ~2.2% of the stock per year, giving the 0.02-0.11 range across interpretations. The paper explicitly recommends against taxing compute in Stage 1 ('exempt AI development; tax AGI entities'), so ramp this lever in only as adoption approaches its AGI-preset ceiling.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0 in BAU and early/Stage-1 years of all presets; >0 only in late AGI-stage presets, or sized to the rent component: ~0.05-0.15 of the saved bill (up to much higher if retained_profit_share is treated as pure rent)
lever_mappings.rationale: Robot taxes map to taxation of reproducible capital (tau_K): 'would distort investment during Stage 1 and are more appropriate for Stage 2'. Since the model's automation_tax_rate is levied on the saved compensation bill out of retained profit, it approximates rent taxation only for the rent-like share of profits; rents are taxable up to ~100%, normal returns at ~4-11%, so blend by assumed rent share (e.g. 100% x rent share x retained_profit_share / saved bill).

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.25-0.375, implemented as tax clawback only (no means-test/work-conditioning crowd-out)
lever_mappings.rationale: Stage-1 revenue converges on broad consumption taxation; a UBI dollar paid out and spent faces the economy-wide effective tax wedge, ~25% of GDP overall and ~37.5% if raised purely as a consumption tax (=0.25/(2/3)). The paper (citing Korinek-Juelfs 2024) argues 'conditioning transfers on work is only desirable if work gives rise to positive utility effects that individuals do not rationally internalize' — i.e., transfers should be unconditional, so model recapture as tax clawback, not means-testing.

lever_mappings.suggested: ~0.04 on federal debt (real); keep consistent with the discount rate used for the AGI-harvesting anchor
lever_mappings.rationale: The paper's key rate, 'the human discount rate—commonly estimated around 4 percent', is the intertemporal price it uses to trade current human consumption against future growth; using the same 4% for the model's federal debt-service rate keeps the preset internally consistent with the compute_effective_rate anchor.

lever_mappings.suggested: Low/binding cap in AGI presets (rate hikes on labor bases cannot close gaps); lean on state_cut_share instead as displacement deepens
lever_mappings.rationale: Maximum labor-tax revenue as a share of output approaches zero as the capital share approaches one — so in high-displacement presets, state income/payroll rate hikes hit a Laffer-style ceiling on a vanishing base. Structurally this says gap-closing should shift toward spending cuts (state_cut_share -> 1) or consumption-style bases the model does not have, rather than allowing unlimited rate hikes.

papers.caveats: This is a normative optimal-taxation primer, not a forecast: the 4% and 11% figures are what a planner *should* levy under specific utility assumptions, not predicted policy, and the 4% applies to Stage-2 AGI capital *stock* taxation — applying it to Stage-1 compute spend contradicts the paper's own guidance that compute/robot taxes distort investment during the transition. tau*_K taxes net capital *income*, a different base from the model's compute-pool lever, hence the wide 0.02-0.11 range. The paper's primary Stage-1 instrument, broad consumption taxation (with a return of differential Ramsey commodity taxation), has no direct lever in the model — ubi_recapture_rate and the existing income/payroll ledger only partially proxy it. Its model uses linear, time-constant taxes plus uniform lump-sum transfers with no displacement dynamics, so it says nothing about ui_weeks, reabsorption, lfp_exit, or federal/state structure. Equity-based pre-Stage-1 instruments it favors (sovereign wealth funds, windfall clauses, Universal Basic Capital) have no lever counterpart. No numeric UBI level is given, so ubi_annual remains unanchored by this paper.

papers.title: Davidson (2023), "What a Compute-Centric Framework Says About Takeoff Speeds" (Open Philanthropy)
papers.verified: Fetched: LessWrong crosspost https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/Gc9FGtdXhK9sCSEYu/what-a-compute-centric-framework-says-about-ai-takeoff and Scott Alexander's summary https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/davidson-on-takeoff-speeds. The canonical report page (openphilanthropy.org, now coefficientgiving.org) returned HTTP 403, so report-internal outputs (e.g. GWP-growth-rate trajectories, %-GDP compute investment at wake-up) could NOT be verified and are omitted. ACX quotes are Scott Alexander's restatement of Davidson's numbers except where he quotes Davidson directly.

key_numbers.quantity: Probability distribution of takeoff duration: time from AI capable of 20% of cognitive tasks (20%-AI) to 100% (100%-AI)
key_numbers.value: ~10% chance <3 months; ~25% <1 year; ~50% <3 years; ~80% <10 years
key_numbers.quote: ~10% to a <3 month takeoff ... ~25% to a <1 year takeoff ... ~50% to a <3 year takeoff ... ~80% to a <10 year takeoff

key_numbers.quantity: Median takeoff duration, 20% of jobs to 100% of jobs
key_numbers.value: ~3 years
key_numbers.quote: From when AI can do 20% of jobs, Davidson expects it will only take about 3 years for it to be able to do 100% of jobs.

key_numbers.quantity: Effective-compute gap between 20%-AI and AGI (100%-AI)
key_numbers.value: 10,000x (~4 orders of magnitude; distribution roughly 1-9 OOMs per ACX)
key_numbers.quote: My best guess, defended in the report, is that you need 10,000X more effective compute to train AGI

key_numbers.quantity: Economic value created at the 20%-AI milestone
key_numbers.value: ~$10 trillion/year added to global GDP (~10% of GWP)
key_numbers.quote: 20%-AI could readily add ~$10tr/year to global GDP

key_numbers.quantity: Median year of human-level AI (full cognitive automation) in the compute-centric framework
key_numbers.quote: CCF says 2043

key_numbers.quantity: Time from AGI (100% cognitive automation capability) to superintelligence
key_numbers.value: 1-12 months (best guess <1 year)
key_numbers.quote: My best guess is that we go from AGI...to superintelligence...in 1 - 12 months.

lever_mappings.suggested: FAST preset: start 0.2 -> end 1.0, with the cumulative ceiling reaching ~0.9-1.0 by year 4-5 of the horizon (front-loaded); sensitivity band: same ramp compressed to 1 year (25th pct) or stretched to 10 years (80th pct)
lever_mappings.rationale: Median 3 years for capability to go 20% -> 100%. Davidson's runtime-compute framing implies deployment tracks capability closely because inference is cheap once training succeeds (ACX: 'you'll have 100 million AGIs doing software research'); adding ~1-2 years of real-world diffusion on top of the 3-year capability median gives a cumulative adoption ceiling hitting ~1.0 by t+4 to t+5. Set the path's start at 0.2 to encode that the scenario clock starts at the 20%-AI milestone.

Davidson's 100%-AI endpoint is defined as AI able to do 100% of cognitive jobs; the 20% starting condition belongs in adoption_path start (0.2), not in feasibility, so the feasibility lever should sit at its ceiling in a Davidson-anchored FAST preset.

20%-AI adds ~$10tr/yr on ~$100tr GWP = +10% of GDP at 20% automation; linear extrapolation to full automation gives +50% of GDP, i.e. passthrough ~0.5 under the lever's definition (full automation of the labor bill -> +this fraction of GDP). Davidson expects super-linearity near full automation (superintelligence within a year), which argues for the top of the range.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.1-0.3 as a horizon average (interpret as high ~0.5 in year 1, falling ~10x/yr)
lever_mappings.rationale: Effective compute per dollar improves ~1 OOM/year in Davidson's framework (ACX: 'Davidson expects this to grow by about an OOM per year for a while'), so the compute cost of replacing a given task falls ~10x/yr; a first-year cost near half of saved compensation averages to ~0.1-0.2 over a 5-10 year horizon. If the model only supports a constant auto_cost, use 0.2.

papers.caveats: Davidson's 20%->100% is TECHNICAL CAPABILITY (tasks AI could do at plausible compute cost), not realized adoption — mapping it directly onto the adoption_path ceiling is the aggressive reading; his own model has deployment lagging capability by under a year only because runtime compute is cheap. All magnitudes are global (GWP), not US. The framework is cognitive-only: it gives no guidance on physical_feasibility or robotics_lag, and nothing on labor-market microstructure (reabsorption_rate, reemployment_haircut, lfp_exit_rate, ui_weeks all unaddressed). Training-compute quantities (10,000x gap, 1e36 FLOP) inform pacing, not any lever directly. The report's GWP-growth-rate outputs could not be fetched (403), so do not cite specific >20%/yr growth claims from this extraction.

lever_mappings.suggested: 3-4 years (FAST preset); 5-6 years if anchoring to the authors' updated medians
lever_mappings.rationale: SC arrives Mar 2027 but mass robot production hits 1M/month only ~1.75 years later (end-2028), after SEZ factory conversion. 1M robots/month = 12M/yr; against ~50-60M US physical-task workers, even sustained peak output needs ~4-5 years to saturate, and production ramps from near zero. A linear ramp-in of 3-4 years approximates this production-constrained substitution; Kokotajlo's 80k framing (conversion ~5x faster than WW2 mobilization, autonomous-economy doubling in weeks only AFTER build-out) supports lag being build-capacity-bound, not capability-bound.

lever_mappings.suggested: 1.0 (reached within ~1-2 years of scenario start)
lever_mappings.rationale: SC (any coding task) Mar 2027 -> SAR Jul 2027 -> ASI ~Apr 2028 median: essentially all cognitive task content becomes technically automatable within ~1 year of the first milestone. For an annual-period model starting at the SC milestone, set cognitive_feasibility = 1.0 and let adoption_path carry the diffusion.

lever_mappings.suggested: start 0.05-0.1 -> end 0.9-1.0, ceiling reached by year 4-6
lever_mappings.rationale: 200,000 copies at 30x speed makes intra-firm cognitive deployment effectively instantaneous once capability exists; economy-wide diffusion is bounded by inference compute and integration ($1T global capex in 2026, scaling fast). The scenario narrative has most of the economy automated by ~2029, i.e. within ~2-3 years of SC; adding slack for the non-frontier economy gives a 0.05 -> 0.9+ cumulative ceiling over 4-6 annual periods.

The race ending's general-purpose robots (1M/month, designed by superintelligent AI, produced in AI-planned SEZs) are depicted doing most manual work; feasibility is high and the binding constraint is production capacity, which is what robotics_lag models. Pair high physical_feasibility with the 3-4 year robotics_lag rather than lowering feasibility.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.3-0.5 in early years, declining toward 0.1-0.2
lever_mappings.rationale: In the scenario's 2026, AI capex ($1T global; OpenBrain compute cost $40B) exceeds AI revenue ($35B for the leader) — investment runs AHEAD of realized wage savings. Once displacement scales, capex of $1-3T/yr against saved US compensation of $3-6T/yr implies auto_cost ~0.3-0.5, falling as algorithmic progress (year-per-month at 10x multiplier) cuts unit inference costs.

papers.caveats: AI 2027 is a single illustrative scenario, not a forecast distribution (only the takeoff supplement gives percentiles, and those are conditional on SC in Mar 2027). The authors' own medians have since slipped ~3-7 years (TED-AI Dec 2030 / Jan 2035), so the scenario's dates should be read as roughly a fast-decile path — appropriate for a FAST preset but not a median. It contains no US fiscal, wage, unemployment, UI, or UBI quantities: nothing maps to reabsorption_rate, reemployment_haircut, lfp_exit_rate, ui_weeks, survivor_* or demand_multiplier. Robot counts convert to worker-equivalents only under an assumed robots-per-worker productivity (I used ~1:1). Much of the SEZ robot output is military/industrial build-out, not labor-substituting service work, so 1M/month overstates the displacement-relevant rate. The 2028-2029 race-ending macro text could not be fully fetched (truncation); one supporting line is verified only via a secondary page and a search snippet.

lever_mappings.suggested: FAST upper end: 0.08-0.15 nominal (vs ~0.04 trend); use 0.06-0.08 if you want %-GDP denominators to stay interpretable
lever_mappings.rationale: Century-in-a-decade = >=10x the rate of technological progress. If technology contributes ~2pp of the ~2% real trend, 10x tech progress implies real growth potential of ~10-20%/yr at the peak; discounting for measured-GDP lag behind 'technological development' and adding ~2% inflation gives a defensible nominal upper band of 8-15%. Warning: this lever feeds %-GDP denominators, so raising it mechanically shrinks reported fiscal gaps as % of GDP.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.9-1.0 (lever ceiling) for the FAST preset
lever_mappings.rationale: Their claim survives conservative assumptions (no software feedback loop, compute scaling stops): >=10x research acceleration means automation's GDP dividend exceeds mere replacement of the saved wage bill. Since the lever caps the dividend at 1.0x the automated labor bill, the intelligence-explosion reading saturates it: set 1.0, with 0.9 as the cautious edge. Anything above the cap must instead show up via baseline_growth_rate.

lever_mappings.suggested: supports n_periods <= 3 years from feasibility to saturation (end ~1.0)
lever_mappings.rationale: AI population growing ~25x/yr and total AI research effort ~10x/yr means that once a task class is feasible, the number of deployable AI worker-equivalents crosses the entire US workforce (~1.6e8) within ~2 years from even a modest base (e.g. 1e6 x 25^2 = 6.25e8), so the cumulative adoption ceiling is compute-permitting within <=3 years; the binding constraint becomes organizational, not capacity.

lever_mappings.suggested: 0.1-0.3 sustained over the horizon
lever_mappings.rationale: Compute headroom of ~10,000x before power limits, with effective compute per dollar improving ~3x/yr from algorithms alone, means automation input spend keeps GROWING in absolute terms while cost per replaced task falls; against a saved wage bill growing with cumulative adoption, a roughly constant 0.1-0.3 share of saved compensation spent on compute is the natural steady-state reading (5x/yr conservative scaling case supports the upper end).

papers.caveats: The paper quantifies research INPUTS (compute, algorithmic efficiency, AI cognitive labor), not GDP, employment, or wages — 'a century's worth of technological development' is not measured GDP growth, so the baseline_growth_rate mapping is a modeling choice, not the authors' claim; they explicitly do not forecast output growth rates. Nothing maps to any labor-market lever (reabsorption_rate, reemployment_haircut, lfp_exit_rate, attrition_rate, ui_weeks), the firm-disposition split, or any tax/transfer lever. All scaling figures are global frontier-AI trends, not US-economy quantities. The 25x/yr AI-population and 500x differential figures describe pre-explosion trend extrapolation, not a post-displacement equilibrium, so using them to pace adoption_path assumes compute is the only bottleneck.

lever_mappings.suggested: RAND-replication preset: one-shot cumulative displacement of 0.10 of the workforce at t=1, flat thereafter (e.g. combined feasibility 0.60 x adoption_path 0.167->0.167; n_periods=10, horizon 2025-2035)
lever_mappings.rationale: Their transitional simulation displaces exactly 10% of workers in a single year with no further shocks: 0.10 / 0.60 feasible share = 0.167 of feasible work adopted immediately. Use their Table 4 2035 values (revenue -~25% nominal under at-cost pricing; corporate revenue 2.8%->0.7% GDP in S4; PIT 14.0%->12.5% GDP in S2) as validation targets for this preset, not as lever inputs.

lever_mappings.suggested: New-jobs scenarios (S1/S3): reabsorption_rate ~= 0.2/yr (full reabsorption within 5 years), reemployment_haircut = 0
lever_mappings.rationale: Unemployment returns fully to baseline after exactly 5 years -> continuous-rate equivalent 1/5yr = 0.2/yr clears the pool on their timetable. They assume reemployment at similar compensation ('Even if displaced workers found other jobs with similar compensation, the temporary disruption... could be comparable to a recession'), so haircut = 0 in this preset.

lever_mappings.suggested: No-new-jobs scenarios (S2/S4): lfp_exit_rate ~= 1.0/yr (immediate exit) with reabsorption_rate = 0
lever_mappings.rationale: The entire 10pp displaced cohort is moved out of the labor force in year 1 (LFPR 62.7%->52.7%) and stays out for the whole 10-year horizon; the fastest our exit channel can replicate that is exit at ~100%/yr for benefit-exhausted workers with zero reabsorption.

lever_mappings.suggested: Monopolistic presets: retained_profit_share ~= 1.0, price_reduction_share ~= 0. At-cost presets: price_reduction_share ~= 1.0 with price_passthrough = 1.0
lever_mappings.rationale: Their pricing axis is exactly our disposition split. At-cost pricing is implemented as core PCE inflation going from +2% to -2% for 10 years (a 4pp/yr deflation swing, calibrated to 1866-1897 industrialization deflation), producing nominal GDP 26% below baseline by 2035 ($32.4T vs $43.9T) - i.e. the full saved bill flows to price cuts that fully deflate the price level.

lever_mappings.suggested: ~0.5-0.6 (the compensation share of GDP) for a RAND-equivalent 'output-neutral' preset
lever_mappings.rationale: They adjust TFP (+11% in S1/S3, +5% in S2/S4) 'so that the net change in output associated with replacing human labor is zero' - i.e. the dividend exactly replaces displaced labor's output contribution and no more. In our units (full automation of the labor bill -> +passthrough x GDP), GDP-neutrality means the dividend equals displaced compensation, so passthrough ~= compensation share of GDP (~0.53), a deliberate lower bound: 'we assume that labor productivity does not increase so that we can produce a lower bound on the revenue changes.'

papers.caveats: Concept mismatches: (1) Revenue outcomes are reported as %-of-GDP at 2035 from a DSGE (FRB/US) with an active Fed monetary response and 60%-replacement transfers - our model has no monetary block, so match their %-revenue-loss targets, not their interest-rate/inflation paths. (2) The deflation channel hits NOMINAL GDP and debt service; our price_passthrough deflates the price level but our ledger is anchored differently - compare in %-GDP space. (3) The 65%/20%/83% ladder is static accounting 'vulnerability' (revenue at risk), not a simulated loss - use as an upper bound, not a point target. (4) Their shock is one-off and rectangular (5y or 10y) rather than a diffusion path, and reemployment carries no wage haircut - so reemployment_haircut, ui_weeks, attrition_rate and survivor_elasticity have no counterpart. (5) No compute tax, automation tax, or state-level fiscal content; their 'AI excise tax' is only a policy suggestion. (6) The 10% displacement level is explicitly arbitrary ('The specific level of 10 percent is arbitrary').

Their scenario descriptions give the cognitive/physical split: Medium = 'Significant improvement at cognitive tasks, but limited physical capabilities'; High = 'Mastery of cognitive tasks plus significant progress in robotics.'

lever_mappings.rationale: Direct 1:1 mapping - their long-run wage scarring is defined identically to our permanent re-employment wage cut (re-employed at 80/70/60% of prior wage). Their 33% transition wage ratio additionally maps onto our service-floor wage during the transition pool (displaced earn 33% of prior wage while searching, taxed at 31% ETR).

lever_mappings.suggested: ~0.03/yr in all three presets (0.10/3y, 0.20/6y, 0.30/9y all annualize to ~0.033/yr of the displaced pool)
lever_mappings.rationale: Permanent exits of 10%/20%/30% of displaced workers accrue over shock lengths of 3/6/9 years: 0.10/3 = 0.033, 0.20/6 = 0.033, 0.30/9 = 0.033 per year - a remarkably stable ~3.3%/yr exit hazard to feed our SSDI channel. Their exits are excluded from benefit spending ('This excludes workers who permanently exit the labour force... from the benefit spending calculation'), whereas ours draw ssdi_annual - a deliberate divergence to document.

lever_mappings.suggested: High-value-capture preset: 0.45 / 0.45 / 0.0 (residual 0.10 split between auto_cost and offshore_share). Low-value-capture preset: 0.15 / 0.15 / 0.0 (residual 0.70)
lever_mappings.rationale: Their three-way allocation of the displaced wage pool (domestic firms / consumers / residual) is our disposition vector: firms = retained_profit_share, consumers = price_reduction_share (consumer surplus from lower prices), survivor raises = 0 (non-displaced wages held constant, flagged as a key uncertainty). The residual is auto_cost x offshore leakage combined: 'the proportions flowing to domestic firms and consumers determine the amount available for taxation... while the residual category does not contribute to domestic tax revenues.'

lever_mappings.suggested: compute_effective_rate: 0.205-0.33, central 0.267 (their capital ETR) if the compute pool is taxed like domestic profits; offshore_share: 0.10 (high capture) to 0.70 (low capture) as an UPPER bound, since their residual bundles untaxed foreign profits with AI operating costs
lever_mappings.rationale: Their capital ETR of 26.7% = average of 20.5% corporate EATR and 33.0% integrated dividend ETR is the best literature anchor for the effective rate on AI-capital income. Foreign-firm payments are taxed at 0% ('treated as Business Profits under the OECD Model... no PE exists, such that the allocation to foreign firms goes untaxed'), which is exactly our offshore_share = untaxed leak. Their ETR sensitivity check is a useful invariant: equalizing labor and capital ETRs at 35% flips low/medium scenarios to revenue gains (+1.53%/+0.42%).

papers.caveats: (1) Calibrated to the 'average OECD country', not the US: tax base is 46.1% labor / 13.75% capital / 31.2% consumption / 8.95% other - the US federal base is far more labor-skewed (84% individual+payroll per RAND) and has no VAT, so their consumption-tax offsets mostly vanish federally and their %-revenue losses likely understate the US case. (2) Results are CUMULATIVE 10-year percent changes vs a static baseline (no GDP growth, no discounting) - convert before comparing to our annual %-GDP series. (3) Pure accounting model: one-off shock, step-change full re-employment when the shock ends, no demand multiplier, no induced losses, no price level (their 'consumers' allocation is untaxed surplus, not our price_passthrough deflation). (4) Benefit spending = 40% of pre-displacement wages for the entire 3-9 year shock - closer to a European replacement scheme than our ui_weeks; their exits get zero benefits vs our ssdi_annual. (5) The 26.7% capital ETR applies to domestic firm profits generally, not to a compute-capital pool; our compute_effective_rate taxes a narrower base. (6) The residual category conflates auto_cost with offshore_share - splitting 10%/70% between the two levers is our judgment call, not theirs. (7) Their High-impact assumptions deliberately exceed the empirical scarring/exit literature ('our assumptions go beyond the range observed in past displacement episodes').

lever_mappings.suggested: GATE-median shape: back-loaded convex ramp - hold ~0.10 for roughly the first decade, then ramp steeply to ~1.0 over the following ~12 years (e.g. start 0.10, end 1.0, n_periods 20-25, with most mass after year 10). lever_mappings.rationale: Unit arithmetic: gap = log10(T) - log10(CT(0)) = 36.5 - 25.7 ~= 10.8 OOM. Automation of the marginal task begins once compute is within Delta-f x gap ~= 5.9 OOM of T, i.e. after (10.8-5.9)/0.5 ~= 10 years at the 0.5 OOM/yr trend; full automation ~10.8/0.5 ~= 22 years out. The paper's Figure 5(e-f) axes (2024-2044, 0-100%) display exactly this family. Note our adoption_path is a share of FEASIBLE work while GATE's f(t) is a share of ALL tasks - with cognitive+physical feasibility set near 1, the two coincide.

lever_mappings.suggested: Combined feasible-now share ~0.10 (range 0.05-0.2) for a GATE-anchored t=0; treat GATE presets as feasibility growing toward 1.0 over the horizon rather than a static ceiling
lever_mappings.rationale: f_init = 0.1 is GATE's estimate of tasks automatable at simulation start (2025), cross-validated against Acemoglu-Restrepo's ~10% task-content displacement over 1987-2017. GATE does not split cognitive vs physical - it is compute-only with no robotics production constraint - so allocate the 0.10 mostly to cognitive_feasibility and treat robotics_lag as exogenous to GATE.

lever_mappings.suggested: GATE-optimistic bound: reabsorption_rate -> high (>=1/yr) with reemployment_haircut = 0 (perfect reallocation, the playground default). GATE-pessimistic bound: reabsorption_rate = 0 and lfp_exit_rate ~= 1/yr (complete displacement = permanent labor-force exit). Use as MC endpoints, not point estimates
lever_mappings.rationale: GATE models no intermediate labor friction: 'perfect reallocation, where workers displaced by automation smoothly transition to remaining non-automated tasks, and complete displacement, where automated workers permanently exit the labor force.' These are exactly the two corners of our (reabsorption_rate, lfp_exit_rate) plane; GATE's own authors flag retraining/reallocation extensions as future work.

lever_mappings.suggested: Positive (complementarity) in GATE-flavored presets; magnitude tied to elasticity of substitution ~0.45-0.87 (rho = -0.65), with survivor_raise_ceiling likely binding at high automation fractions
lever_mappings.rationale: With tasks as gross complements (sigma < 1), non-automated tasks become the bottleneck: 'The lower this parameter, the less substitutability there is between tasks and the stronger the bottleneck effect exerted by non-automated tasks.' Bottlenecked human tasks command rising marginal product -> survivors' market wage rises with automation, the opposite sign of a slack-driven substitution story - i.e. survivor_elasticity > 0.

lever_mappings.suggested: auto_cost: ~0.3-0.7 of the saved bill flowing to compute in aggressive GATE presets (initial compute investment $200B/yr default, rising toward tens of percent of GWP in simulations). productivity_passthrough: >=1.0 for GATE-like explosive-growth presets (their GWP-growth-vs-automation panel spans 0% to 1000% growth), vs our conservative sub-1 defaults
lever_mappings.rationale: GATE's default initial compute investment is $200B/yr and its Figure 5(b) axis for 'Predicted investment in compute-related capital' runs to 50% of GWP; with a ~45% baseline labor share ('This corresponds to a labor share of around 45%'), compute spend relative to the displaced labor bill lands roughly at 0.2-1.1, hence auto_cost 0.3-0.7 as a loose anchor. On output: GATE's growth outcomes at high automation vastly exceed the displaced wage bill, so any GATE-consistent preset needs productivity_passthrough at or above 1 - useful as the optimistic edge of our MC range, not a central value.

papers.caveats: (1) GATE computes a fundamentally different output: global GWP growth under optimal-planner investment, with NO fiscal block - no taxes, government, UI, or revenue concept - so it provides adoption-shape and elasticity anchors only, no revenue validation targets. (2) Its automation fraction is a share of TASKS (which enter symmetrically), not workers or wage bill; mapping to our headcount-based displacement assumes uniform task-worker weights. (3) The trajectory is endogenous - compute investment responds to automation returns - so the 'adoption path' shifts with parameters; my year arithmetic uses the exogenous 0.5 OOM/yr historical trend and is only indicative (the paper publishes no calibrated year-by-year baseline table in text; figures are illustrative). (4) Defaults are extremely uncertain: T spans 8 OOMs (1e33-1e41), and the FLOP-gap default is internally inconsistent (0.55 in Table 9, 0.52 in the derivation bullet). (5) No robotics/embodiment channel, so robotics_lag and physical_feasibility have no GATE analog. (6) Wages, prices and interest rates are explicitly deferred to future work ('quantities of interest such as prices, wages and interest rates'), so the survivor_elasticity mapping is inferred from the production structure, not from reported wage paths. (7) The complete-displacement case removes workers from the labor force permanently with no transition pool - it bounds but does not calibrate reabsorption_rate, ui_weeks, or reemployment_haircut.
